proposals.title: Spatiotemporal Decoupling of Precipitation Regimes and Vegetation Productivity in the Pyrenees: A Multi-Scale Analysis of Drought Extremes and Phenological Shifts (1984-2024)
proposals.motivation: Recent studies confirm a Mediterraneanization trend in the Iberian Peninsula, characterized by reduced spring-summer precipitation and increased frequency of intense drought extremes. The Pyrenees, a critical biodiversity hotspot and water tower, are highly vulnerable, yet a spatially continuous, long-term assessment of how these specific precipitation regime shifts—beyond mean annual changes—affect the productivity of key forest and grassland ecosystems is lacking. This gap hinders the development of targeted adaptation strategies for mountain regions under climate change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the observed reduction in spring-summer rainfall and intensification of extreme drought events has led to a progressive decoupling between precipitation availability and vegetation productivity in Pyrenean ecosystems, with grasslands showing higher sensitivity and faster phenological adjustments than forests.
proposals.method: We will employ a spatially explicit process-based analysis using long-term (1984-2024) satellite-derived vegetation indices (NDVI, EVI from Landsat and MODIS) and high-resolution gridded climate data (SPI at multiple timescales, SPEI). The core method involves: 1) Calculating annual productivity metrics (e.g., seasonal integrals, peak values) and phenological metrics (start/end of season) from harmonic analysis of time series. 2) Applying distributed lag models and breakpoint detection algorithms to quantify non-linear vegetation response to precipitation deficits and extreme drought years. 3) Conducting spatial change detection analysis to identify areas where the correlation between precipitation regimes and productivity has significantly weakened over time.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first spatially continuous, multi-decadal quantification of vegetation sensitivity to specific precipitation regime shifts in the Pyrenees. It will directly contribute to improving dynamic vegetation models by parameterizing drought response thresholds for major mountain biomes. The findings will offer empirical evidence to inform regional climate change impact assessments and forest management policies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond correlating productivity with annual drought indices to explicitly dissect the impact of seasonal precipitation loss and discrete extreme events on phenological cycles, using high-resolution spatial analysis to reveal ecosystem-specific vulnerability patterns not apparent in coarse-scale studies.

proposals.title: Comparative Geoecological Pressure in Mediterranean Spain: Historical Cultivation vs. Modern Tourism and Infrastructure
proposals.motivation: In Mediterranean regions like Spain, the narrative of peak human pressure during historical agricultural expansion is being challenged by modern land-use changes. Understanding whether contemporary drivers like intensive tourism and linear infrastructure exert comparable or greater pressure on geoecological systems is critical for sustainable land management and climate adaptation, especially in drought-prone areas.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spatial footprint and intensity of geoecological pressure from modern tourism and infrastructure development in selected Mediterranean regions of Spain equal or exceed the pressure exerted by widespread historical cultivation during the 19th century.
proposals.method: We will employ a spatially explicit, multi-temporal analysis. Historical pressure (19th century) will be reconstructed using land-use maps from historical archives and cadastral data. Modern pressure (post-1980s) will be quantified using satellite-derived land cover (Landsat, Sentinel-2), night-time lights, and road/urban infrastructure datasets. Geoecological response will be assessed using long-term vegetation indices (NDVI from AVHRR and MODIS, 1983-2025) and drought indices (Standardized Precipitation-Evapotranspiration Index, SPEI). A comparative spatial analysis will quantify pressure extent, intensity, and correlation with vegetation/drought trends.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, spatially continuous comparison of human pressure epochs, directly linking them to long-term vegetation and drought dynamics. It will advance the field of drought impacts by integrating historical land-use legacies with modern anthropogenic stressors, offering a novel framework for assessing cumulative geoecological vulnerability.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, data-driven comparison of two distinct anthropogenic epochs using spatially continuous satellite archives and historical reconstructions, moving beyond qualitative narratives to test the specific claim of 19th-century peak pressure in a Mediterranean context.

proposals.title: Disentangling the Climatic Drivers of Altitudinal Succession: Temperature and Precipitation Forcing of Vegetation Homogenization and Heterogenization in Mediterranean Mountains
proposals.motivation: Recent satellite-based studies, including those by Vicente-Serrano, reveal complex altitude-dependent patterns of vegetation succession, with processes of both homogenization (increasing similarity) and heterogenization (increasing divergence) in phenology and greenness. In the context of intensifying climate change and extreme drought events in Mediterranean regions like Spain, understanding the specific climatic drivers—temperature warming versus precipitation changes—behind these divergent ecological trajectories is critical for predicting future ecosystem resilience and biodiversity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the observed altitudinal patterns of vegetation homogenization and heterogenization are primarily driven by differential sensitivities to climatic trends: homogenization at higher altitudes is forced by temperature warming, while heterogenization at mid-to-low altitudes is a nonlinear response to increasing aridity and drought intensity.
proposals.method: We will employ a spatially continuous analysis using long-term (1983-2025) satellite-derived vegetation indices (e.g., NDVI, EVI) to map succession variability metrics (e.g., temporal trend in spatial variance) across altitudinal gradients in Iberian mountain ranges. These will be statistically linked to high-resolution gridded climate data (temperature, precipitation, and the Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index, SPEI) using spatial panel regression models and process-based attribution. The analysis will specifically test for breakpoints and nonlinear responses in the vegetation-climate relationship across altitudes using generalized additive mixed models (GAMMs).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, climate-attributed framework for interpreting complex satellite-observed vegetation dynamics, moving beyond description to causation. It will directly inform vulnerability assessments by identifying the altitude zones and ecosystem types most sensitive to specific climate drivers, thereby refining regional climate change impact projections for Mediterranean mountain ecosystems.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has documented altitudinal gradients in vegetation change, this proposal novelly frames these changes explicitly as processes of homogenization/heterogenization and directly tests their attribution to competing climate drivers (temperature vs. water balance), a critical and unresolved question in mountain biogeography.

proposals.title: Continental-scale assessment of agricultural abandonment dynamics in European mountains using long-term NDVI variability (1984-2025)
proposals.motivation: Agricultural abandonment in European mountains is a widespread land-use change with profound impacts on biodiversity, ecosystem services, and rural socio-economics. Pre-2026 studies are often regional, lacking a consistent, spatially-explicit continental analysis. A pan-European assessment is critical to identify common drivers, rates, and ecological consequences, informing EU agricultural and environmental policies like the Common Agricultural Policy and the EU Biodiversity Strategy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the magnitude and spatial patterns of long-term NDVI variability provide a robust, scalable indicator of agricultural abandonment intensity across Europe's major mountain ranges, revealing distinct continental gradients linked to climatic and socio-economic factors.
proposals.method: We will apply a standardized methodology, building on Vicente-Serrano's previous work on Spain. Using the full Landsat 5-8 and Sentinel-2 archive (1984-2025), we will calculate annual integrated NDVI and its inter-annual variability (coefficient of variation) at a 30m resolution for all European mountain areas (defined by the EU's mountain typology). Abandonment will be identified via a significant temporal breakpoint (Pettitt test) towards increased variability, indicating a shift from managed to unmanaged vegetation. Validation will use LUCAS land cover data, national statistics, and high-resolution imagery.
proposals.contribution: This will produce the first high-resolution, spatially continuous map of agricultural abandonment dynamics across all European mountains, quantifying rates and extents over four decades. It will establish a standardized framework for monitoring land abandonment, directly contributing to EU Green Deal monitoring and providing a baseline for modeling future ecosystem changes.
proposals.novelty: While NDVI trends are commonly used, this proposal uniquely scales up the specific methodology of using NDVI *variability* as a abandonment indicator—previously demonstrated in Spain—to a continental analysis, integrating multi-sensor data across diverse biogeographic regions to uncover pan-European patterns.

proposals.title: Divergent Pathways of Ecosystem Recovery: Assessing the Impact of Active Reforestation vs. Natural Succession on Vegetation Resilience and Spatial Diversity in Mediterranean Abandoned Lands
proposals.motivation: Land abandonment and active reforestation are widespread land-use changes in Mediterranean Europe, driven by socio-economic shifts and climate policy. However, their long-term impacts on ecosystem structure, particularly spatial diversity and resilience to extreme droughts, remain poorly quantified and compared. Understanding these divergent recovery pathways is critical for informing effective land management and restoration strategies under increasing climatic aridity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that actively reforested areas will exhibit lower spatial diversity in vegetation structure (NDVI) and greater vulnerability to drought-induced degradation over time compared to naturally regenerating fields, due to the monospecific, even-aged stands typical of plantations versus the heterogeneous, multi-species mosaics of natural succession.
proposals.method: We will analyze the spatio-temporal evolution of vegetation using a dense time series of Landsat and Sentinel-2 NDVI data (1985-2025) for paired sites of active reforestation and natural succession in a representative Mediterranean region (e.g., Iberian System). We will compute annual spatial metrics of NDVI diversity (e.g., standard deviation, texture indices) and phenological metrics. Drought impact will be assessed by coupling these trends with the Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI) at multiple timescales, using correlation and breakpoint detection analyses to identify resilience thresholds.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, spatially explicit comparison of two major land-cover change trajectories, directly contributing to debates on optimal restoration ecology. It will quantify trade-offs between recovery speed, structural diversity, and drought resilience, offering evidence-based criteria for climate-adaptive land management policies in drought-prone areas.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have examined abandonment or reforestation separately, this proposal directly contrasts their long-term ecological outcomes using high-resolution spatial diversity metrics and integrates this with a robust drought vulnerability analysis, a synthesis not yet fully addressed in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Conceptual Drought and Shifting Baselines: A Methodological Framework for Reconstructing Pre-Cartesian Metaphysical Diversity
proposals.motivation: In climate science, 'shifting baseline syndrome' describes the loss of awareness of past ecological states, hindering our understanding of environmental change and extremes. Similarly, in intellectual history, the dominance of the Cartesian *cogito* may have created a 'conceptual drought,' obscuring the rich diversity of premodern metaphysical frameworks. This project adapts methods from spatial ecology and historical climatology to systematically recover this suppressed diversity, challenging the narrative of inevitable philosophical progress.
proposals.hypothesis: The Cartesian *cogito* functioned as a dominant paradigm that induced a 'conceptual aridification,' outcompeting and rendering invisible a more diverse 'ecosystem' of premodern metaphysical concepts. By applying adapted ecological reconstruction methods, we can recover specific, marginalized ontological commitments and argumentative structures.
proposals.method: We adapt the spatial-temporal analysis of ecological change to textual corpora. Using a curated database of pre-1650 and post-1650 philosophical texts, we will apply: 1) **Conceptual Index Calculation**: Develop a normalized 'Metaphysical Diversity Index' (MDI) based on the frequency and co-occurrence networks of key ontological terms (e.g., substance, form, soul, being). 2) **Change Detection Analysis**: Use statistical breakpoint detection and trend analysis (e.g., Mann-Kendall test) on MDI time series to identify periods of significant conceptual loss. 3) **Spatial-Analogy Mapping**: Treat different philosophical schools/traditions as 'areas' and trace the 'extent' and 'intensity' of Cartesian conceptual dominance over time, visualizing the contraction of alternative frameworks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, methodological framework for assessing paradigm shifts in intellectual history, moving beyond qualitative narrative. It will produce a first-order reconstruction of key elements of the pre-Cartesian metaphysical 'landscape' that were marginalized, offering a new baseline for understanding modernity's philosophical foundations.
proposals.novelty: It directly transposes quantitative methods from the study of ecological drought and vegetation change—core to the researcher's expertise—to the domain of conceptual history, creating a novel interdisciplinary analytical toolkit. This approach treats philosophical concepts as measurable, spatially distributed entities subject to processes of dominance and extinction.

proposals.title: Mapping the Drought of Meaning: A Spatio-Temporal Analysis of Metaphysical Absence as a Constraint on Aesthetic Form in Post-1990 European Art
proposals.motivation: In climate science, the absence of precipitation (drought) is not a void but an active driver of ecological and social processes, studied through indices like the SPEI. Similarly, in contemporary aesthetics, the widely noted absence of premodern metaphysical frameworks (e.g., cosmic order, teleology) is treated as a cultural 'drought,' yet its causal role in shaping specific formal artistic responses remains poorly mapped and understood. This research addresses this gap by applying spatial-temporal diagnostic methods from environmental science to cultural analysis, treating absence as an operative constraint.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific, historically situated metaphysical absences function as generative constraints that predictably shape the emergence of distinct aesthetic techniques (e.g., non-linear narrative, material indeterminacy) in post-1990 European art, analogous to how drought indices predict vegetation response.
proposals.method: We will develop a 'Negative Archaeology Index' (NAI), adapting the Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI) methodology. The NAI will quantify the 'intensity' and 'duration' of specific metaphysical absences (e.g., teleology, symbolic correspondence) over time using textual analysis of philosophical and critical discourse from 1990-2020 as a proxy. This spatio-temporal dataset will be correlated, using change detection and spatial regression techniques, with a georeferenced database of artistic techniques cataloged from major European biennials and exhibitions. The goal is to identify significant statistical relationships between periods of high 'metaphysical drought' and the prevalence of specific formal solutions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, diagnostic framework that moves beyond qualitative lament or celebration of postmodernity to empirically model the link between philosophical-historical conditions and aesthetic production. It will introduce a novel, quantifiable method for cultural analysis, bridging environmental science and humanities. The resulting NAI offers a tool for explaining the emergence and spatial diffusion of contemporary art genres.
proposals.novelty: While the 'end of grand narratives' is a well-established concept, this research is novel in treating specific absences as measurable, spatially variable constraints with diagnostic predictive power for artistic form, applying rigorous climatological drought-index methodologies to a core problem in aesthetics.

proposals.title: The Aesthetics of Absence: Quantifying the Generative Role of Drought-Induced Vegetation Loss in Shaping Landscape Patterns and Ecological Memory
proposals.motivation: Drought research has predominantly framed vegetation loss as a deficit, focusing on negative impacts on ecosystem services and productivity. This deficit-centric view overlooks the potential for absence to be a generative force, reorganizing spatial patterns and ecological memory in ways that may influence resilience and future aesthetic-perceptual responses to landscapes. Bridging physical drought metrics with perceptual-aesthetic theories offers a novel, transdisciplinary challenge in environmental science.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that periods of severe drought, conceptualized as 'productive absences,' generate distinct, persistent spatial signatures in vegetation patterns that correlate with subsequent recovery trajectories and can be linked to formal aesthetic qualities (e.g., complexity, coherence) in the landscape.
proposals.method: We will apply a spatial-process framework using long-term satellite-derived vegetation indices (e.g., NDVI, 1983-2025). For major drought events identified by the Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI), we will quantify pre- and post-drought spatial patterns using lacunarity analysis and variograms to measure structural changes. A change vector analysis will track the trajectory of recovery. These geospatial metrics will be analyzed as potential correlates of aesthetic dimensions derived from landscape perception studies, using multivariate statistical models.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new theoretical lens ('productive absence') to drought impact studies, moving beyond purely deficit-based assessments. It will provide a quantitative, spatially-explicit methodology to link ecological disturbance with aesthetic theory, offering a framework for evaluating how extreme climatic events fundamentally reshape both ecological and human perceptual systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the conceptual framework of drought impact from 'loss' to 'generative restructuring' and in the technical integration of geospatial change detection with qualitative aesthetic theory, a synthesis not previously attempted in climatological studies.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Deceptive Vitality of Literary Characters: A Hydraulic Proxy Model for Modern Subjectivity
proposals.motivation: In climate science, the concept of 'deceptive vitality' in vegetation—where plants maintain greenness via deep reserves while their core hydraulic systems are failing—is critical for understanding drought impacts and ecosystem collapse. This research proposes that analogous processes define modern subjectivity, where characters in literature outwardly perform coherent selves while their internal 'hydraulic' systems of meaning and connection are in irreversible decline. Synthesizing ecological models with literary analysis offers a novel, quantifiable framework for diagnosing a core condition of modernity, moving beyond purely qualitative interpretation.
proposals.hypothesis: Key characters in modern and postmodern literature function as hydraulic proxies, where measurable patterns in their dialogue, action, and narrative context reveal a decoupling between outward narrative vigour (sapwood) and the collapse of internal, sustaining systems (heartwood), analogous to ecological drought responses.
proposals.method: We will adapt methods from spatial drought analysis, treating a character's textual representation as a time series. Using NLP techniques (e.g., sentiment analysis, topic modeling, and cohesion metrics on dialogue/narration), we will compute a 'Character Hydraulic Index' (CHI). This index will quantify the divergence between surface-level coherence metrics (sapwood proxy) and deeper semantic/relational metrics (heartwood proxy) across the narrative arc. The analysis will be applied to a curated corpus of 20th-century novels, with statistical change-point detection (e.g., Pettitt test) used to identify irreversible collapse points in the internal system.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, transdisciplinary methodology for literary analysis, introducing quantifiable metrics for character decay. It will formalize the ecological analogy of deceptive vitality as a critical tool for cultural diagnosis, offering a new model for understanding modern subjectivity. The project will also demonstrate the exportability of spatial-process and index-based methods from environmental science to the digital humanities.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct operationalization of a precise ecological model (hydraulic failure/deceptive vitality) as an analytical framework for literary character, moving beyond metaphor to create testable, computational indices. This synthesis of quantitative drought science methodologies with philosophical-literary questions is unprecedented in pre-2026 research.

proposals.title: Detecting Cultural Die-Off: A Remote Sensing Framework for Quantifying Literary Phenology Loss in Modernizing Regions
proposals.motivation: The study of cultural loss during modernization is often qualitative and localized, lacking scalable, empirical methods to detect and quantify systemic change. Concurrently, in environmental science, remote sensing provides robust, spatially continuous methods to detect phenological shifts and ecosystem stress, such as drought-induced forest die-off. This project bridges these domains to create a novel, evidence-based methodology for diagnosing cultural absence at scale, addressing a critical gap in understanding the parallel impacts of global change on ecological and cultural systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The temporal decline of traditional literary features (e.g., specific genres, motifs, stylistic markers) in large-scale text corpora follows detectable patterns analogous to vegetation phenology loss, and these patterns can be identified using anomaly detection algorithms adapted from remote sensing of drought impacts.
proposals.method: We will construct diachronic corpora for a case study region (e.g., Arabic literature 1850-1950). Textual features (genre labels, thematic n-grams, syntactic complexity indices) will be extracted and aggregated into annual time series. Following Vicente-Serrano's work on drought and phenology, we will apply Breaks For Additive Seasonal and Trend (BFAST) algorithm for breakpoint detection and Seasonal-Trend decomposition using LOESS (STL) to identify critical transition points. The magnitude and rate of feature decline will be quantified using analogs of remote sensing vegetation indices, treating literary 'health' as a composite metric.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, transferable computational framework for macro-scale cultural analysis, directly importing validated environmental science methods into the digital humanities. It will generate the first quantitative, longitudinal maps of literary feature loss for a selected region, providing empirical evidence for theories of cultural modernization. The project will also demonstrate the utility of phenological concepts for modeling non-biological systems of change.
proposals.novelty: The direct conceptual and methodological translation of remote sensing phenology and drought detection techniques to the analysis of literary history is unprecedented. It moves beyond simple trend analysis in digital humanities by applying specific algorithms (BFAST, STL) designed to detect non-gradual, ecosystem-level transitions in noisy temporal data.

proposals.title: Spatio-temporal analysis of literary absence: Quantifying thematic drought in non-Western canons confronting modernity
proposals.motivation: Recent computational literary studies have pioneered the analysis of 'absence'—thematic or lexical voids—in the works of Shakespeare and Cervantes as a response to early modernity. However, this framework remains untested in non-Western literary traditions, which experienced distinct, often colonial, modernities. Applying Vicente-Serrano's expertise in quantifying environmental absences (drought) to cultural data can reveal how different literary systems register and respond to profound historical disruption.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the 'absence' framework will reveal distinct, quantifiable patterns of thematic depletion and emergence in non-Western literary canons (e.g., Arabic, Japanese, or South Asian) during their encounters with modernity, patterns that differ systematically from the Eurocentric models established in prior work.
proposals.method: We will adapt spatial drought analysis methods to a literary corpus. For selected authors (e.g., Natsume Sōseki, Rabindranath Tagore, Tayeb Salih), we will construct a high-dimensional semantic vector space from pre-modern reference texts (pre-1850). Using time-series analysis of word embeddings from their key works, we will compute a Standardized Thematic Absence Index (STAI), analogous to the Standardized Precipitation Index, to identify periods of significant departure from the semantic 'climate normal.' Change-point detection algorithms will then map the spatial (i.e., across thematic domains) and temporal evolution of these absences.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, quantitative methodology for comparative literary studies, moving beyond Eurocentric models of modernity. It will generate the first empirical maps of thematic change in non-Western literary traditions, offering a data-driven perspective on cultural adaptation and loss. It extends the application of geospatial climate methodologies to the digital humanities.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the transdisciplinary application of rigorous climatological drought indices and spatial change processes to the analysis of literary history, specifically targeting a gap in non-Western literary scholarship. It treats thematic absence not as a metaphor but as a quantifiable, spatio-temporal phenomenon to be tracked.

proposals.title: Generative Concealment in the Anthropocene: Mapping the Aesthetic Dimensions of Drought as a Structured Absence
proposals.motivation: In the context of accelerating climate change and increased frequency of extreme drought events, purely physical or meteorological indices fail to capture the full societal and cultural impact of water scarcity. The dominant scientific narrative frames drought as a deficit, but its profound influence on human experience, perception, and cultural production remains underexplored. This gap limits our holistic understanding of drought's role in shaping environmental consciousness and artistic response.
proposals.hypothesis: The conceptual framework of 'generative concealment'—where a structured absence (like the missing water in a drought) creates conditions for new forms of perception and expression—can be operationalized to analyze and map the socio-ecological and aesthetic dimensions of drought. We hypothesize that regions experiencing long-term ecological drying exhibit correlated shifts in cultural narratives and aesthetic motifs that move beyond simple loss towards generative adaptation.
proposals.method: We will employ a spatial-analytical framework, fusing long-term remote sensing data (e.g., NDVI time series from 1983-2020 to track vegetation phenology and drought impacts) with curated textual/artistic corpora. Using the Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI) to define drought events and their spatial processes, we will create a multi-decadal geodatabase of drought intensity and extent. Concurrently, we will apply natural language processing and thematic analysis to regionally-specific literary, journalistic, and artistic works from corresponding periods, using algorithms for topic modeling (e.g., Latent Dirichlet Allocation) and sentiment analysis to quantify aesthetic and narrative shifts. Spatial statistics will test for correlations between physical drought metrics and emergent cultural themes.
proposals.contribution: This work will pioneer a novel, transdisciplinary methodology for linking biophysical drought processes with cultural analysis. It will produce a spatially continuous map of 'drought aesthetics,' revealing how prolonged environmental stress generates distinct forms of expression and meaning. The results will offer environmental humanities a rigorous empirical tool and provide climate scientists with a deeper framework for communicating drought's societal dimensions.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in its direct, quantitative fusion of geospatial drought science with computational humanities to test a philosophical concept ('generative concealment'), moving beyond disciplinary case studies to produce spatially explicit, evidence-based correlations between ecological change and cultural production.

proposals.title: Quantifying Climatic Negation: A Spatio-Temporal Analysis of Vapor Pressure Deficit Extremes and Forest Mortality as an Aesthetic of Absence
proposals.motivation: Recent research has established vapor pressure deficit (VPD) as a key physiological driver of drought stress and forest mortality globally. However, its impact is often studied through positive metrics of change. This project addresses the need to conceptualize and quantify the 'negating' force of extreme atmospheric aridity—the catastrophic removal of habitable conditions—and its visible, aesthetic imprint on landscapes, a dimension critical for communicating ecological risk.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that spatio-temporal patterns of extreme VPD, analyzed as a negating climatic force, will show a stronger statistical coupling to observed large-scale forest die-off and vegetation browning than standard drought indices, and that the resulting spatial patterns of mortality can be meaningfully interpreted through a framework of aesthetic negation.
proposals.method: We will employ a spatially continuous, multi-decadal (1983-2025) analysis over forested areas, primarily in Spain and other Mediterranean hotspots. Using ERA5 reanalysis for VPD and long-term satellite NDVI/vegetation optical depth trends, we will: 1) Calculate annual maxima and exceedance frequencies of a high VPD threshold; 2) Develop a novel 'Climatic Negation Index' integrating the intensity, duration, and spatial extent of VPD extremes; 3) Use spatial correlation and change-point detection algorithms to link VPD negation events to subsequent vegetation decline; 4) Apply landscape metrics to quantify the fragmentation and pattern of die-off as an aesthetic signature of loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new quantitative framework linking atmospheric physiology to large-scale ecological mortality through the lens of negation. It will produce a novel geospatial index of climatic negation risk and offer a transdisciplinary bridge between physical climatology, landscape ecology, and environmental humanities, enriching the discourse on drought impacts.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in reframing VPD extremes not merely as a positive stressor but as an apophatic, negating force, and in formally proposing the spatial patterns of resultant forest mortality as a quantifiable 'aesthetic of absence'—a secular revelation of climatic limits.

proposals.title: Temporal Downscaling of Character Crisis: A Dendrochronological Framework for Mapping Intra-annual Psychological Stress in Shakespeare's Hamlet
proposals.motivation: In climate science, high-resolution temporal analysis of drought events, using indices like the SPEI, reveals critical details about onset, peak intensity, and recovery that are masked at annual scales. Similarly, in literary studies, character crises are often analyzed as monolithic events, obscuring the nuanced temporal dynamics of psychological stress. This project bridges these domains to develop a rigorous, temporally explicit framework for character analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying principles of temporal downscaling and event characterization from drought science to a literary character's trajectory will reveal distinct, quantifiable phases of stress accumulation, peak, and recovery, analogous to the intra-annual growth response of a tree to drought.
proposals.method: We will treat the dramatic text as a temporal series. First, we define a character-specific 'stress index' based on linguistic and action-based markers (e.g., soliloquy density, decisiveness metrics, interaction aggression) quantified per scene/segment. Using change-point detection algorithms (e.g., PELT) and peak-over-threshold analysis, we identify the onset, duration, and termination of discrete 'crisis events.' The temporal structure of these events is then characterized using metrics from drought analysis (e.g., magnitude, intensity, recovery rate) to create a 'xylogenesis of character' timeline.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, transferable methodological framework for high-resolution narrative analysis, moving beyond thematic interpretation to temporal physiology. It will provide a concrete, data-driven model of Hamlet's crisis structure, offering a new lens for character study and demonstrating the fertile synergy between environmental science and digital humanities.
proposals.novelty: The direct translation of dendrochronological and drought event characterization methodologies to literary analysis is unprecedented. It reframes character study not as thematic interpretation but as the analysis of a dynamic, time-series process of stress and response.

proposals.title: Phylogenetic mapping of aesthetic drought: a pan-European analysis of literary modernism's spatio-temporal emergence
proposals.motivation: Understanding the spread of cultural phenomena like modernism is akin to tracking the spatio-temporal dynamics of environmental processes. Pre-2026, studies of literary modernity often focus on single traditions or binary influences, lacking a continent-wide, quantitative model that distinguishes between shared heritage, diffusion, and independent convergence. This gap mirrors challenges in climatology where distinguishing the drivers of regional drought patterns requires robust spatio-temporal analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: The emergence of modernist aesthetics across European literatures follows identifiable spatio-temporal pathways that can be modeled phylogenetically, revealing distinct clusters of diffusion from common sources (homology) versus regions of independent, convergent innovation (analogy) under similar socio-historical pressures.
proposals.method: We will adapt computational phylogenetic methods (e.g., maximum parsimony, neighbor-joining algorithms) used in evolutionary biology to literary history. A core set of 'aesthetic markers' (e.g., narrative fragmentation, thematic alienation, specific metric breaks) will be coded as discrete character states from a curated corpus of texts across major European languages (1880-1950). Using spatial analysis tools (GIS), we will map the inferred phylogenetic trees onto geographic and temporal axes, analyzing the 'change' in aesthetic traits over 'years' and across 'areas' to model diffusion 'processes' and identify 'extreme' hubs of innovation or resistance.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative, spatio-temporal model of modernist development across Europe, moving beyond qualitative comparative studies. It will provide a novel methodological framework for analyzing cultural evolution and contribute a detailed 'map' of aesthetic 'drought' (traditional persistence) and 'rainfall' (innovation), offering insights into how cultural climates change.
proposals.novelty: The direct application of phylogenetic algorithms and spatial change detection to model the evolution of a large-scale literary movement is novel. It treats aesthetic traits as analogous to genetic markers and environmental variables, creating a dynamic synthesis of digital humanities and geospatial analytical techniques prevalent in pre-2026 environmental science.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Spatiotemporal Dynamics of Cultural Modernity: A Drought-Index Inspired Framework for European Literary Basins
proposals.motivation: Current studies of European literary modernity often rely on national or linguistic canons, lacking a spatially explicit framework to analyze its diffusion and intensity as a geographically conditioned process. This gap mirrors pre-2026 challenges in climatology, where understanding drought required moving beyond point measurements to spatially continuous assessments of intensity, duration, and impact across complex terrains. Applying geospatial analytical methods to cultural data can reveal how 'aesthetic aridity' or 'modernist flowering' propagate through defined cultural basins.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatiotemporal patterns of literary modernization across Europe can be modeled and compared by treating major cultural regions as basins, where the intensity and timing of modernist traits exhibit dynamics analogous to hydrological droughts, constrained by local 'geocultural' factors versus transnational influences.
proposals.method: We adapt the Standardized Precipitation-Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI) methodology to cultural data. For each basin (e.g., Danube, Baltic, Apennines), a time series (e.g., 1880-1950) will be constructed using a 'Modernity Index' derived from textual analysis (via NLP topic modeling) of a corpus of literary works, quantifying the prevalence of modernist themes. This series will be fitted to a log-logistic distribution to calculate a Standardized Modernity Index (SMI), identifying periods of significant aesthetic 'deficit' or 'surplus'. Spatial interpolation techniques (e.g., kriging) will map these patterns, and change detection algorithms will analyze shifts between 'current' (observed) and 'potential' (transnationally influenced) modernity states.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, spatially continuous framework for comparative literary history, moving beyond qualitative regional studies. It will generate novel maps of modernist intensity and identify 'hotspots' and 'refugia' of tradition, offering a systemic model of cultural change. It establishes a transferable methodology for applying geoscientific indices to complex cultural processes.
proposals.novelty: The proposal's novelty lies in the direct translation of a robust climatological drought-index framework (SPEI) and spatial change detection processes to the domain of literary history, creating a quantifiable, comparative metric for cultural phenomena previously analyzed only qualitatively.

proposals.title: A Comparative Philology of Ontic Givenness: Premodern Conceptual Ecologies and Modern Metacritique
proposals.motivation: Contemporary environmental and drought studies often rely on modern scientific ontologies that frame nature as an object of analysis, potentially obscuring historical and cultural understandings of environmental phenomena. Reconstructing premodern metaphysical frameworks is crucial for a more nuanced, transhistorical dialogue about human-environment relationships, which can inform current debates on ecological perception and crisis. This research addresses a gap in the historical epistemology of environmental science.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic philological reconstruction of core ontological terms (e.g., 'physis', 'natura', 'dharma', 'dao') within specific premodern textual traditions will reveal distinct, internally coherent ecologies of 'givenness' that resist reduction to modern binaries. This recovered complexity will demonstrate the historical specificity and potential limitations of modern/postmodern ontological stances centered on absence.
proposals.method: The research employs a three-stage comparative philological methodology. First, a detailed conceptual analysis of key ontological terms will be conducted within delimited textual corpora (Greco-Roman, Medieval Scholastic, early Indian philosophical, and pre-Qin Chinese). Second, semantic networks and conceptual maps will be constructed for each tradition to visualize internal ecologies and relationships. Finally, a structured comparative dialogue will be initiated between these models using a framework of 'gradients of givenness' to articulate a spectrum of premodern stances, which will then be used for a metacritical interrogation of modern ontological categories.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a refined, transhistorical vocabulary for discussing ontological commitments, moving beyond simplistic premodern/modern dichotomies. It will contribute a historically-grounded metacritique to philosophical and environmental humanities discourses, challenging the universality of absence-focused frameworks. The project will also deliver a replicable methodology for the comparative analysis of metaphysical concepts across traditions.
proposals.novelty: The approach avoids imposing modern categories by starting from ground-up philological analysis within traditions, rather than applying a pre-defined comparative grid. It uniquely bridges detailed historical-conceptual recovery with active, critical engagement with contemporary theoretical paradigms in the environmental humanities.

proposals.title: A Spatio-Temporal Analysis of Literary Drought: Mapping the Dissolution of Ontological Presence in European Modernity
proposals.motivation: Current studies of literary modernity often focus on the presence of 'absence' (e.g., secularization, alienation) as a defining feature, but lack a quantitative, comparative framework to trace its emergence. This project addresses this gap by conceptualizing the loss of premodern metaphysical certainty as a 'drought' of ontological presence, requiring systematic spatial and temporal mapping. Drawing on pre-2026 advances in corpus linguistics and spatial humanities, it proposes a novel application of quantitative change detection to large-scale literary history.
proposals.hypothesis: The dissolution of premodern ontological presence in European literature did not occur uniformly, but followed distinct spatio-temporal patterns and 'drought pathways' that can be modeled by tracking the declining frequency and co-occurrence of specific semantic and formal features associated with metaphysical certainty.
proposals.method: We will construct a diachronic, multi-lingual corpus of European literary texts from 1300-1900. Using pre-2026 NLP techniques (e.g., word embeddings, topic modeling, syntactic parsing), we will first define a composite 'Presence Index' based on the stable co-occurrence networks of concepts like divine agency, cosmic order, and symbolic coherence in a premodern baseline period. We will then apply spatial statistical methods and change-point detection algorithms (similar to those used in climatology for drought onset) to track the index's decline across time and national/linguistic regions, identifying periods of abrupt change, slow decay, and spatial diffusion.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirically grounded, comparative topography of modernity's emergence in European literature, moving beyond qualitative, nation-specific studies. It will introduce a robust methodological framework from environmental science to the digital humanities, enabling the diagnosis of complex cultural shifts as spatio-temporal processes.
proposals.novelty: It inverts the standard analytical focus from 'absence' to the mapped recession of 'presence,' and innovatively adapts methods for analyzing climatic droughts—tracking the onset, intensity, and spatial spread of a deficit—to model a fundamental epistemological shift in cultural history.

proposals.title: Assessing the Validity of Static Potential Vegetation Maps in a Changing Climate: A Spatio-Temporal Analysis of Succession Lags and Disturbance Legacies in Iberian Ecosystems
proposals.motivation: Static potential vegetation (PV) maps, which assume long-term climatic equilibrium, are foundational for ecological modeling and land-use planning. However, in a context of rapid climate change and increasing disturbance regimes (e.g., droughts, fires), the assumption of equilibrium is questionable. This mismatch may lead to significant errors in predicting ecosystem responses, biodiversity shifts, and drought vulnerability, particularly in Mediterranean regions like Spain where Vicente-Serrano's work is focused.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that observed vegetation patterns across Iberia systematically deviate from static PV map predictions due to time-lagged ecological succession and persistent legacies of past disturbances (e.g., fires, land abandonment), and that these deviations are spatially correlated with recent climatic trends and drought history.
proposals.method: We will integrate long-term satellite-derived vegetation phenology data (e.g., NDVI time series from 1983-2020) with high-resolution climatic datasets (including the Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index, SPEI) and historical disturbance maps (fire perimeters, land-use change). Using spatial statistical models (e.g., geographically weighted regression) and change detection algorithms (e.g., Breaks For Additive Seasonal and Trend, BFAST), we will quantify the spatio-temporal discrepancy between observed vegetation states (from satellite trends) and the static PV map. We will then model these discrepancies as a function of climatic water balance trends, drought extremity, and disturbance history.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a quantitative, spatially explicit framework to evaluate the validity of PV maps under non-equilibrium conditions. It will directly contribute to improving dynamic vegetation models and drought impact assessments by incorporating ecological memory and succession lags. The findings will offer critical insights for adaptive ecosystem management and conservation planning in climate-sensitive regions.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies note PV map limitations, this work novelly quantifies the deviation using multi-decadal satellite phenology and explicitly links it to pre-2026 metrics of climatic change and disturbance legacy, moving beyond theoretical critique to an empirical, process-based assessment grounded in spatial analysis of extreme events.

proposals.title: Landscape-scale hydraulic constraints on forest drought resilience: a multi-basin analysis of xylem anatomical thresholds in the Pyrenees
proposals.motivation: Drought-induced forest decline is accelerating globally, yet predictive models often rely on correlative climate-vegetation relationships that lack a mechanistic physiological basis. Understanding how landscape-scale environmental filters shape key functional traits, like xylem anatomy, is critical to forecast forest persistence under increasing aridity. This bridges a major gap between cellular-scale hydraulic safety and regional-scale forest dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that generalizable thresholds in xylem anatomical traits (e.g., tracheid lumen diameter, wall thickness) exist across tree populations and that these thresholds are systematically shaped by basin-scale environmental gradients, thereby controlling landscape patterns of drought resilience.
proposals.method: We will sample dominant tree species across multiple Pyrenean basins with contrasting climatic water balance and forest cover. Using quantitative wood anatomy on tree cores, we will measure key tracheid traits (e.g., lumen area, cell wall thickness) via image analysis software like ROXAS. These anatomical data will be integrated with pre-2026 spatial datasets (topography, soil moisture, SPEI drought indices) using generalized additive mixed models (GAMMs) to identify trait-environment relationships and critical anatomical thresholds linked to observed forest persistence or dieback.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, trait-based framework for understanding landscape-scale forest vulnerability, moving beyond correlative bioclimatic models. It will deliver quantifiable xylem anatomical thresholds that can be integrated into dynamic vegetation models to improve predictions of drought-induced mortality under climate change.
proposals.novelty: This study uniquely scales quantitative wood anatomy from individual trees to a multi-basin landscape comparison, explicitly testing for generalizable hydraulic constraints. It directly links measurable xylem traits to spatial patterns of forest resilience derived from remote sensing, a synthesis not yet achieved in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Projecting Climate-Driven Shifts in Potential Vegetation Biomes and Drought Vulnerability in the Mediterranean Basin (2025-2100)
proposals.motivation: Potential vegetation maps, which depict the natural landscape in the absence of human activity, are foundational for ecological baselines and land-use planning. However, existing maps for regions like the Mediterranean Basin are based on historical climate norms and do not account for anthropogenic climate change, particularly the intensification of drought extremes. This limits our ability to anticipate future ecosystem boundaries and vulnerabilities, crucial for conservation and adaptation strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by the end of the 21st century, climate change projections will drive a significant northward and upward elevational shift in potential vegetation biomes across the Mediterranean Basin, with arid and semi-arid zones expanding substantially due to increased drought frequency and intensity.
proposals.method: We will employ a spatial process-based approach, updating the classic Holdridge life zone classification scheme using high-resolution (approx. 1 km) climate projections from the latest CMIP6 multi-model ensemble under SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5 scenarios. Key bioclimatic variables (mean temperature, precipitation, potential evapotranspiration) for the periods 2041-2070 and 2071-2100 will be calculated. The Standardized Precipitation-Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI) at 12- and 24-month scales, a core drought index in the researcher's work, will be integrated to quantify changes in drought stress within each projected life zone, providing a continuous assessment of biome-specific aridity changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first dynamic, climate-change-informed potential vegetation maps for the Mediterranean Basin, quantifying the projected magnitude and spatial patterns of biome shifts. It will directly link these shifts to changes in drought regimes, offering a novel, process-based vulnerability assessment that identifies future ecological 'hotspots' most at risk from aridification.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have modeled species distributions or vegetation indices under climate change, this proposal uniquely focuses on updating the fundamental concept of 'potential vegetation' itself by integrating state-of-the-art climate projections and a robust drought index (SPEI) into a classic bioclimatic classification framework, moving beyond correlative models to a more mechanistic, drought-process-informed projection.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Impact of Landscape Diversity Loss on Drought-Regulating Ecosystem Services in Mediterranean Regions
proposals.motivation: In Mediterranean regions like Spain, increasing drought frequency and intensity (pre-2026) threaten ecosystem stability and water security. Landscape diversity, particularly in vegetation composition and structure, is hypothesized to be a critical buffer against drought impacts by modulating key ecosystem services like water regulation and soil retention. However, the measurable translation of biodiversity differences between current (often degraded) and potential natural landscapes into changes in these services remains poorly quantified at regional scales.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that landscapes with lower diversity relative to their potential natural state exhibit a significantly reduced capacity to provide drought-regulating ecosystem services, leading to amplified hydrological and vegetation stress during extreme dry periods.
proposals.method: We will employ a spatially explicit, multi-source framework. First, we will map current and potential natural landscape diversity using pre-2026 satellite-derived phenology metrics (e.g., NDVI time-series from MODIS/Landsat) and species distribution models. Second, we will quantify key drought-related ecosystem services (e.g., runoff control, soil moisture retention) using process-based indices like the Reconnaissance Drought Index (RDI) and soil water balance models. Finally, we will apply spatial statistical models (e.g., geographically weighted regression) and change-point detection algorithms to analyze the relationship between the diversity deficit (current vs. potential) and the magnitude of ecosystem service change during documented extreme drought years (e.g., 2005, 2012, 2017).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, spatially continuous assessment of how biodiversity loss compromises ecosystem resilience to drought. It will deliver quantitative metrics linking landscape structure to service provision, offering actionable insights for land-use planning and restoration prioritization. The findings will bridge the gap between ecological drought studies and ecosystem service valuation in a changing climate.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, spatially explicit coupling of the 'diversity deficit' concept with process-based drought and ecosystem service indices, moving beyond correlative studies to a mechanistic, landscape-scale understanding of vulnerability.

proposals.title: Inverse Modeling of Land Use Intensity to Maintain Target Landscape Diversity in Mediterranean Drylands Under Climate Extremes
proposals.motivation: In Mediterranean regions like Spain, increasing drought frequency and intensity threaten landscape diversity, a key component of ecosystem resilience. Current land management often reacts to degradation rather than proactively planning to sustain diversity. An inverse approach, determining the land use intensity needed for a desired diversity state, is crucial for adaptive management under climate change, aligning with pre-2026 EU biodiversity and agricultural policy goals.
proposals.hypothesis: For a given target level of landscape diversity (e.g., measured by spectral or habitat heterogeneity), there exists an optimal, non-linear relationship with human land use intensity that can be quantified, and this relationship is modulated by historical drought regimes.
proposals.method: We will leverage long-term satellite data (Landsat, MODIS) from 1985-2025 to derive annual maps of landscape diversity using Rao's Q index on vegetation indices (NDVI, EVI) and land use intensity from Corine Land Cover and agricultural statistics. A Bayesian hierarchical model will be developed to invert the causal relationship, treating target diversity as input and land use intensity as the output. The model will integrate drought indices (Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index, SPEI) as covariates to assess how extreme dry years alter the optimal land use intensity for maintaining diversity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, operational framework for land-use planning that defines sustainable intensity thresholds to conserve landscape heterogeneity. It will advance the theoretical understanding of the human-nature coupling in dryland systems by formally inverting the causality typically studied. The resulting models can directly inform regional agricultural and conservation policies aimed at climate adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While most studies correlate land use change with biodiversity loss, this proposal inverts the problem to solve for the management driver given an ecological target, a novel application of inverse modeling in landscape ecology. It uniquely integrates long-term drought dynamics as a core modulator of this relationship, bridging Vicente-Serrano's expertise in drought processes with spatial landscape analysis.

proposals.title: Landscape Memory to Drought: Assessing Temporal Scaling of Vegetation Response Across Pyrenean Land-Abandonment Gradients
proposals.motivation: In mountainous regions like the Pyrenees, widespread land abandonment is a dominant driver of landscape change, altering vegetation structure and ecosystem function. Understanding how these new landscapes respond to climatic extremes, particularly drought, is critical for predicting future ecosystem resilience and water resources. A key unresolved question is how the legacy of past land use, embedded in different stages of abandonment, modifies the temporal scaling of vegetation's response to water deficit.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that landscapes at later stages of abandonment (i.e., more advanced secondary succession) exhibit a longer 'memory' to drought, with vegetation response integrated over longer temporal scales, due to deeper root systems and more complex community structures. Conversely, recently abandoned or managed areas will show faster, short-scale responses tied to meteorological drought.
proposals.method: We will apply a space-for-time substitution across a stratified gradient of abandonment stages in the Spanish Pyrenees, mapped using historical land-use data and recent high-resolution imagery. For each stage, we will compute the Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI) at multiple timescales (1-24 months) from 1983-2020. Vegetation response will be characterized using long-term NDVI and phenological metrics (start/end of season) from Landsat and AVHRR data. The relationship between SPEI and vegetation indices will be analyzed using cross-correlation and wavelet coherence to identify the dominant temporal scale of coupling for each abandonment class.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework linking socio-ecological transitions (abandonment) to the fundamental temporal scaling of drought impact. It will quantify how land-use legacy modulates ecosystem memory, refining vulnerability assessments. The results will directly inform regional climate adaptation strategies by identifying which landscape types are most sensitive to short-term vs. long-term drought.
proposals.novelty: While drought-vegetation relationships are well-studied, explicitly testing how the temporal scale of this coupling varies systematically along a human-induced land-cover succession gradient is novel. It integrates Vicente-Serrano's expertise in drought indices and spatial processes with the emerging question of abandonment impacts on ecological memory.

proposals.title: A Phenomenological Remote Sensing Framework for Interpreting Forest Die-Off: Bridging Spectral Anomalies and Landscape Hermeneutics in Mediterranean Ecosystems
proposals.motivation: Mediterranean forests face escalating drought-induced die-off, typically analyzed through spectral indices like NDVI. However, purely quantitative thresholds fail to capture the complex, place-specific meaning of ecological absence for human communities and policy. An interdisciplinary approach is needed to enrich the interpretation of remote sensing data, linking ecological change to cultural and narrative dimensions of landscape.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a structured phenomenological reduction (epoché) to satellite-derived phenological time series will reveal patterns and meanings of forest die-off that are obscured by standard anomaly detection, generating a hermeneutic layer that enhances ecological communication and understanding.
proposals.method: We will use long-term (1983-2020) satellite data (e.g., AVHRR, MODIS) to compute phenological metrics and anomalies in Spanish forests. A systematic 'bracketing' protocol will be applied: first identifying spectral absences (e.g., persistent negative NDVI anomalies), then iteratively suspending prior ecological assumptions to analyze the spatiotemporal patterns of these absences as primary phenomena. This will involve spatial clustering of absence events and temporal pattern analysis, treating the results as a 'text' for hermeneutic interpretation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel methodology that adds a qualitative, interpretative layer to remote sensing drought and die-off studies, improving science communication. It will also demonstrate a concrete, empirical application of phenomenological philosophy, grounding it in spatially explicit environmental data.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in explicitly fusing Husserlian phenomenology with remote sensing time-series analysis to interpret ecological absence, moving beyond index-based thresholds to a hermeneutic reading of landscape change.

proposals.title: Narrative Epidemiology: A Dual-Framework Applying Survival Analysis to Drought-Induced Forest Mortality and Literary Character Trajectories
proposals.motivation: Forest mortality due to intensified drought is a critical ecological and socio-economic threat, yet communicating the complex, cumulative nature of tree death to the public remains challenging. Concurrently, the human experience of existential threat is a core theme in literature, structured by authors in ways that may parallel quantitative risk models. Bridging these domains can enhance both ecological communication and narrative theory.
proposals.hypothesis: The temporal dynamics of tree mortality under compounding climatic stressors can be formally modeled using the same survival analysis frameworks (Cox proportional hazards, competing risks) that describe the narrative trajectories of literary characters facing existential crises, revealing shared structures of risk and resilience.
proposals.method: We will apply Cox and Fine-Gray competing-risks models to a high-resolution spatiotemporal dataset of tree mortality in drought-prone Spanish forests, using pre-2026 remote sensing (e.g., MODIS phenology, Vicente-Serrano's SPEI drought index) and forest inventory data. In parallel, we will code a corpus of 19th-20th century literary texts to construct character 'survival' datasets, where 'death' is an existential endpoint (literal or metaphorical). Model outputs (hazard ratios, cumulative incidence functions) from both domains will be analyzed for structural parallels and used to define narrative archetypes (e.g., 'the resilient elder') for ecological communication.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel interdisciplinary methodology, 'narrative epidemiology,' that makes complex ecological hazard models more legible to non-specialists through narrative framing. It will also provide a formal, quantitative lens for literary analysis, testing whether narrative construction follows probabilistic risk structures.
proposals.novelty: The direct, formal application of identical survival analysis models to both ecological and literary data, treating trees as characters and characters as actuarial subjects, is unprecedented. This fusion moves beyond metaphor to create a shared analytical framework for temporal threat.

proposals.title: Pan-Mediterranean Assessment of Climate-Induced Conifer Die-off: A Multi-Species, Multi-Site Analysis of Vulnerability and Bioclimatic Drivers
proposals.motivation: Recent extreme droughts have triggered widespread, but spatially heterogeneous, mortality events in Mediterranean conifer forests, threatening ecosystem services and carbon stocks. However, existing studies are largely local or species-specific, limiting our understanding of regional-scale vulnerability patterns and the key climatic and physiological thresholds across diverse species and climates. A systematic, pan-regional assessment is urgently needed to inform adaptive forest management under climate change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that conifer die-off across the Mediterranean Basin is driven by specific, quantifiable interactions between drought intensity (measured by climatic indices) and species-specific bioclimatic niches, and that these mortality events exhibit coherent spatial patterns linked to recent climatic trends.
proposals.method: We will establish a standardized network of die-off observation sites across the Mediterranean, leveraging existing forest health inventories and remote sensing. Time-series of vegetation indices (e.g., NDVI, EVI from Landsat and MODIS) from 1985-2025 will be analyzed to detect mortality events and their timing. These will be correlated with high-resolution climatic drought indices (Standardized Precipitation-Evapotranspiration Index, SPEI) at multiple timescales. A spatial analysis of change will employ machine learning (e.g., Random Forest) to model die-off probability as a function of multi-year drought metrics, species distribution, topography, and soil characteristics.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first spatially continuous, multi-species map of recent conifer die-off vulnerability for the Mediterranean region. It will identify critical drought duration-intensity thresholds for major conifer species and quantify the relative role of climatic water deficit versus heat stress across bioclimatic gradients, directly informing vulnerability assessments and conservation prioritization.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated, pan-regional scale of analysis, moving beyond case studies to a systematic comparative framework that explicitly links spatially continuous drought dynamics with multi-species physiological responses across the entire Mediterranean climate gradient.

proposals.title: Spatiotemporal Dynamics of Post-Drought Vegetation Recovery: Identifying Functional Traits and Environmental Drivers of Resilience in Iberian Forests
proposals.motivation: Research on drought impacts has predominantly focused on vulnerability, decline, and mortality, creating a critical knowledge gap regarding the processes of recovery in surviving ecosystems. Understanding the physiological traits and environmental conditions that facilitate post-drought resilience is essential for predicting forest dynamics under increasing climate extremes. This inverse problem approach shifts the paradigm from studying loss to understanding survival and recovery mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that post-drought recovery trajectories in surviving trees are not random but are systematically controlled by a combination of species-specific physiological traits (e.g., hydraulic safety margins, carbon storage capacity) and local environmental gradients (e.g., soil water holding capacity, microclimate).
proposals.method: We will leverage long-term satellite data (e.g., MODIS, Landsat) to derive high-resolution recovery indices, such as the rate of return of Enhanced Vegetation Index (EVI) to pre-drought baselines after major drought events identified by the Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI). These spatiotemporal recovery maps will be integrated with in-situ trait databases (TRY, Iberian functional trait databases) and high-resolution environmental layers (soil, topography, climate) using spatial statistical models and machine learning (e.g., Random Forest regression) to identify the key predictors of recovery speed and completeness.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first spatially continuous assessment of recovery resilience for Iberian forests, moving beyond mortality maps. It will quantitatively link satellite-observed recovery patterns to plant functional traits, providing a mechanistic framework to forecast which forest stands are most likely to recover from future droughts. The results will directly inform forest management and conservation strategies aimed at enhancing ecosystem resilience.
proposals.novelty: While extensive work exists on drought onset and impact, systematically modeling the *recovery phase* as an inverse problem—using remote sensing to infer the traits and conditions that enable it—represents a novel and necessary shift in perspective. It integrates Vicente-Serrano's expertise in drought indices and spatial processes with emerging trait-based ecology to answer a fundamentally different question.

proposals.title: Disentangling the Relative Influence of Socioeconomic and Natural Disturbance Drivers on Vegetation Dynamics in Mediterranean Drylands (1983-2025)
proposals.motivation: Recent literature often emphasizes socioeconomic factors (e.g., land abandonment, agricultural intensification) as primary drivers of vegetation change in Mediterranean regions. However, the increasing frequency and intensity of natural disturbances—droughts, wildfires, and pest outbreaks—pose a significant, yet under-quantified, competing control. A systematic, spatially explicit assessment is needed to challenge the assumed primacy of socioeconomic drivers and clarify the role of extreme events in shaping ecosystem trajectories.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in Mediterranean drylands of Spain, the spatiotemporal patterns of vegetation productivity and phenology anomalies are more strongly correlated with the occurrence and legacy of natural disturbances (extreme drought, wildfire, disease) than with long-term socioeconomic trends, particularly in areas with high climatic sensitivity.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-source spatial analysis for 1983-2025. Vegetation dynamics will be characterized using long-term NDVI/LAI time series from Landsat and MODIS, applying breakpoint detection (BFAST) and trend analysis (Theil-Sen slope). Disturbance events will be mapped using the Standardized Precipitation-Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI) for drought, MODIS MCD64A1 for fire perimeters, and forest health databases. Socioeconomic drivers will be represented via CORINE Land Cover change layers and census data. The relative contribution of each driver will be quantified using spatial panel regression models and variance partitioning techniques across different ecological regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework to rank the drivers of vegetation change, directly testing competing paradigms in land change science. It will deliver high-resolution maps identifying regions where natural disturbances override socioeconomic signals, offering critical insights for climate adaptation and ecosystem management strategies under increasing climatic extremes.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies analyze single drivers, this proposal integrates drought (SPEI), fire, and biotic disturbances into a unified spatial framework to directly compete them against socioeconomic factors, leveraging long-term satellite phenology to attribute change causality over four decades.

proposals.title: Post-2000 Dynamics of Alpine Revegetation in the Spanish Pyrenees: Assessing the Role of Drought Intensification and Land-Use Change
proposals.motivation: Mountain ecosystems like the Pyrenees are critical biodiversity hotspots and water towers, highly sensitive to climate change. Pre-2026 studies documented widespread revegetation and shrub encroachment since the mid-20th century, linked to rural abandonment and warming, but suggested a potential deceleration in the 1990s. It is crucial to determine the post-2000 trajectory, as this period is characterized by intensified drought extremes and warming, which may have reversed or altered previous greening trends, impacting ecosystem services and hazard regimes (e.g., fire, erosion).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the post-2000 revegetation rate in the Spanish Pyrenees has spatially diverged, with widespread deceleration or reversal (browning) in drought-sensitive, south-facing slopes at lower elevations due to increased aridity, while acceleration (greening) continues in more mesic, high-elevation areas where thermal constraints are relaxed.
proposals.method: We will employ a spatially continuous analysis using the full Landsat (5, 7, 8) and Sentinel-2 archives (1984-2025) to compute annual peak-season vegetation indices (NDVI, EVI). A multi-index framework, including the Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI) at various timescales, will quantify drought intensity and frequency. Trend analysis (Mann-Kendall, Theil-Sen slopes) will be applied to annual vegetation metrics and climatic water balance for three periods: 1984-2000, 2001-2025, and the full record. Statistical modeling (e.g., spatial panel regressions) will attribute vegetation changes to climatic droughts, temperature trends, and static factors (elevation, aspect, historical land use).
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first comprehensive, high-resolution assessment of post-2000 vegetation dynamics across the entire Spanish Pyrenees, directly testing the deceleration hypothesis. It will quantify the role of recent drought intensification versus legacy land-use changes, offering critical insights for predictive models of mountain ecosystem response to climate change. The findings will inform regional adaptation strategies for biodiversity conservation, water resource management, and pastoral land planning.
proposals.novelty: While past work identified early revegetation trends, this study uniquely focuses on the 21st-century shift into a regime of intensified climatic extremes, using a dense satellite time series and a robust drought index (SPEI) to disentangle the evolving drivers of alpine ecosystem change beyond the initial abandonment signal.

proposals.title: Disentangling climate and socioeconomic drivers of post-abandonment revegetation in the Spanish Pyrenees (1984-2025): A multi-sensor remote sensing and drought index analysis
proposals.motivation: The Pyrenees have undergone intense land abandonment and subsequent vegetation recovery, a process often attributed to socioeconomic change. However, concurrent climate warming and altered drought regimes may be accelerating or modifying these patterns, with critical implications for mountain ecosystem services, carbon sequestration, and hydrological cycles. A rigorous, spatially explicit quantification separating these drivers is lacking, limiting our ability to project future landscape trajectories under continued climate change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that observed greening trends in the Spanish Pyrenees are not solely driven by land-use abandonment, but are significantly modulated and accelerated by climate change, particularly through warming-induced lengthening of the growing season and changes in the frequency and intensity of drought events.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-sensor remote sensing approach, using Landsat (1984-2025) and MODIS time series to derive long-term vegetation indices (NDVI, EVI) and phenological metrics (start/end of season). These will be analyzed against high-resolution climate data (temperature, precipitation) and the Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI) at multiple timescales to capture drought impacts. A spatial panel regression framework, incorporating socioeconomic covariates (e.g., historical land-use maps, population density), will be used to statistically partition the variance in vegetation trends attributable to climatic versus socioeconomic factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative assessment of the relative contribution of climate change to revegetation dynamics in the Pyrenees, moving beyond qualitative narratives. It will deliver high-resolution maps identifying areas where climate, rather than abandonment, is the dominant driver, offering a novel template for similar studies in other mountain regions.
proposals.novelty: While past studies document greening, this research innovatively integrates long-term drought indices (SPEI) and phenological metrics within a spatial econometric model to explicitly isolate the climate change signal from the well-known land-use abandonment signal, a critical step for attribution.

proposals.title: Spatiotemporal coupling of shrub encroachment dynamics and plant diversity changes in Iberian rangelands: a multi-scale assessment driven by drought extremes
proposals.motivation: Shrub encroachment is a widespread ecological transition in Mediterranean rangelands, driven by land abandonment and climate change, particularly increasing drought frequency and intensity. This process threatens the high biodiversity value of open pastoral landscapes, yet a spatially explicit, long-term understanding of how encroachment rates and patterns directly impact plant species diversity is critically lacking. This gap hinders effective conservation and land management strategies in these socio-ecological systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the rate and spatial pattern of shrub encroachment, modulated by the temporal sequence of drought events, non-linearly controls changes in plant species richness and composition, with threshold effects leading to accelerated diversity loss beyond specific encroachment levels.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-scale remote sensing approach. First, we will generate annual, high-resolution (30m) shrub cover fraction maps for Iberian rangelands (1984-2025) using Landsat data and a Random Forest regression model trained on Sentinel-2 and aerial imagery. Encroachment dynamics (rate, patchiness) will be characterized using spatial metrics. Second, we will link these maps to a curated database of plant species inventories using a space-for-time substitution framework.
