The role of drought will be quantified using the Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI) at multiple timescales, analyzing how drought sequences precede and accelerate encroachment phases.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first spatially continuous, decadal-scale quantification of the biodiversity impact of shrub encroachment in Iberia, directly linking land cover change processes to diversity metrics. It will advance the mechanistic understanding of how climate extremes drive vegetation transitions and their ecological consequences, offering a transferable framework for monitoring and forecasting biodiversity change in dynamic landscapes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, fine-scale spatiotemporal coupling of process-based shrub encroachment metrics (not just static cover) with observed plant diversity data, explicitly testing for non-linear and threshold responses conditioned by drought history, moving beyond simple correlative studies.

proposals.title: Disentangling the Drivers of Deforestation in Tropical Mountains: A Spatio-Temporal Analysis of Agricultural Intensification and Climatic Extremes
proposals.motivation: While extensive research has focused on revegetation following land abandonment, tropical mountain regions undergoing agricultural intensification face severe deforestation threats, impacting biodiversity, carbon stocks, and hydrological cycles. Pre-2026 studies highlight the vulnerability of these areas to compound pressures from expanding agriculture and increasing climatic extremes, yet the specific spatio-temporal processes and interactions driving deforestation remain poorly quantified. This gap limits effective land-use planning and conservation strategies in these critical ecosystems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deforestation in tropical mountains is not a uniform process but is driven by the non-linear interaction of specific agricultural drivers (e.g., pasture expansion, perennial crop cultivation) and amplified by the occurrence of meteorological and agricultural droughts, which reduce ecosystem resilience and facilitate land-cover change.
proposals.method: We will apply a methodological inversion of revegetation studies by using long-term satellite data (Landsat, MODIS) to derive annual land-cover maps and vegetation indices (e.g., NDVI) from 1990-2025. Deforestation events will be identified using breakpoint detection algorithms (e.g., BFAST). We will then employ spatial statistical modeling (e.g., geographically weighted regression, GWR) and panel data analysis to quantify the relative influence of dynamic drivers, including agricultural suitability, market access, population density, and drought indices (Standardized Precipitation-Evapotranspiration Index, SPEI), on the observed deforestation patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a high-resolution, spatially explicit diagnosis of deforestation drivers in tropical mountains, moving beyond correlative maps to identify causal processes. It will directly contribute to the field of drought impacts by quantifying how climatic extremes interact with human pressure to trigger land-cover change. The results will offer a transferable framework for assessing deforestation risk under future climate and socio-economic scenarios.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly inverting the well-established methodological framework for studying land abandonment and revegetation to instead diagnose active deforestation processes, and in rigorously testing the synergistic role of agricultural drought as a catalyst for permanent land-cover conversion in intensifying landscapes.

proposals.title: Quantifying Non-Additive Interactions Between Climate Warming and Land Cover Change on Drought Severity and Vegetation Response in Mediterranean Regions
proposals.motivation: Current assessments of drought drivers often treat climatic and land-use changes as additive, independent forcings. This simplification may critically misrepresent ecosystem vulnerability, particularly in Mediterranean regions like Spain, where warming coincides with afforestation and agricultural abandonment, potentially creating synergistic feedbacks that amplify water stress. A framework to disentangle these interactions is essential for accurate drought risk projection and land management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the effect of climate warming on atmospheric drought (via increased evaporative demand) and ecological drought (via vegetation response) is non-linearly amplified in areas that have experienced increased vegetation density or cover change, leading to greater water deficit than predicted by additive models.
proposals.method: We will employ a spatially explicit, observation-based framework for Spain (1984-2020). Using long-term satellite data (e.g., AVHRR, MODIS), we will derive vegetation indices (NDVI, EVI) and phenological metrics as response variables. Key predictors will include the Standardized Precipitation-Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI) at multiple timescales, land cover change trajectories from CORINE, and climate data (E-OBS). The core analysis will use Generalized Additive Mixed Models (GAMMs) with tensor product smooths to explicitly model the non-additive interaction between temperature/SPEI and land cover change indices on vegetation greenness and drought recovery.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel empirical quantification of climate-land cover synergies in drought intensification, moving beyond attribution studies. It will deliver a methodological framework to identify hotspots where land management could either mitigate or exacerbate climate-induced aridification. The results will directly inform regional adaptation strategies in water-limited ecosystems.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have separately analyzed climate and land use effects on drought, this proposal explicitly models their statistical interaction as a non-linear, spatially varying process, challenging the prevailing additive assumption in drought driver attribution.

proposals.title: Drought Resilience and Ecosystem Functionality in Post-Agricultural Woodland Expansion: A Spatiotemporal Analysis of Mediterranean Land Abandonment
proposals.motivation: Agricultural abandonment in Mediterranean Europe is often framed as a loss of cultural landscape and a degradation of ecosystem services. However, this perspective may overlook the potential for natural ecosystem recovery and rewilding, particularly under increasing drought frequency and intensity driven by climate change. A rigorous, spatially explicit assessment of how post-abandonment vegetation dynamics relate to drought resilience is critically needed to inform land management and conservation policies.
proposals.hypothesis: Woodland expansion following agricultural abandonment in Mediterranean Spain leads to increased ecosystem resilience to drought over multidecadal timescales, as evidenced by improved vegetation health metrics and hydrological regulation compared to actively managed agricultural lands during extreme dry periods.
proposals.method: We will employ a spatiotemporal analysis using long-term (1984-2025) Landsat and MODIS time series to map land abandonment and subsequent woodland expansion across representative regions of Spain. Vegetation phenology (start/end of season, peak productivity) and drought response will be quantified using the Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI) at multiple timescales and satellite-derived indices (NDVI, EVI). We will apply change-point detection algorithms and spatial correlation analyses to compare the drought sensitivity (e.g., resilience metrics based on recovery time and resistance indices) of expanding woodlands against persistent agricultural areas and natural forests, controlling for topographic and edaphic factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, evidence-based framework to evaluate whether land abandonment represents an ecological degradation or a recovery pathway. It will directly contribute to debates on rewilding and natural climate solutions by linking land-use change trajectories to ecosystem drought vulnerability. The findings will offer critical insights for regional adaptation strategies in water-limited environments.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond simple land-cover change detection to explicitly test the functional ecological outcome—drought resilience—of a major socio-ecological transition. It challenges the entrenched negative narrative of abandonment by applying rigorous hydro-climatological and phenological metrics central to Vicente-Serrano's research, within the specific context of Mediterranean ecosystem recovery.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating the ecological baseline: The role of traditional agropastoral landscapes in modulating drought vulnerability and vegetation resilience in Iberian drylands
proposals.motivation: In Mediterranean regions, traditional agropastoral landscapes are often implicitly used as a natural baseline for assessing ecological degradation and drought impacts. However, these landscapes are themselves the product of long-term anthropogenic disturbance, raising critical questions about their suitability as a reference for 'natural' ecosystem function and resilience. This is particularly urgent under climate change, where understanding true ecological baselines is key for effective conservation and land management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the structural and functional characteristics of vegetation in traditional agropastoral landscapes, shaped by historical human use, represent a distinct ecological state with a specific drought response signature, differing significantly from both intensively managed modern landscapes and potential natural vegetation states.
proposals.method: We will leverage long-term satellite data (1983-2020) to derive vegetation indices (e.g., NDVI) and drought indices (Standardized Precipitation-Evapotranspiration Index, SPEI) at high spatial resolution. Using land-use change maps, we will stratify areas into three categories: persistent traditional agropastoral, modern intensive, and natural/semi-natural. We will apply spatial statistical methods and breakpoint detection algorithms to compare time series of vegetation sensitivity to drought (using resilience metrics like resistance, recovery, and hysteresis) across these strata, controlling for climatic gradients.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, evidence-based framework for questioning the use of cultural landscapes as an ecological baseline in dryland studies. It will advance the understanding of how historical land-use legacies mediate contemporary ecosystem responses to climate extremes, offering critical insights for land-use planning and restoration targets in drought-prone areas.
proposals.novelty: While much research quantifies drought impacts on vegetation, few studies explicitly challenge the baseline assumption embedded in these assessments by disentangling the climatic signal from the profound, persistent legacy of traditional human disturbance regimes on ecosystem function.

proposals.title: Projecting Post-Abandonment Land Cover Trajectories in the Pyrenees: Integrating Climate Extremes and Socioeconomic Scenarios to Model Scrub-to-Forest Succession
proposals.motivation: Agricultural abandonment in European mountain regions like the Pyrenees triggers secondary succession, altering ecosystem services, fire regimes, and carbon sequestration. The rate and spatial pattern of this scrub-to-forest transition are poorly quantified and are critically modulated by increasing climatic extremes, particularly drought, and future socioeconomic decisions. Understanding these dynamics is essential for regional adaptation planning and biodiversity conservation under global change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that future land cover trajectories in abandoned Pyrenean areas will exhibit strong non-linearities and spatial heterogeneity, where succession rates are primarily constrained by drought frequency and intensity, and secondarily modulated by scenario-dependent land management policies.
proposals.method: We will employ a spatially explicit modeling framework integrating: 1) A multi-temporal land cover classification (1984-2024) from Landsat and Sentinel-2 to derive historical succession rates using change detection algorithms. 2) A process-based model linking the Standardized Precipitation-Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI) at multiple timescales, and other remote sensing indices (e.g., NDVI), to annual transition probabilities. 3) Projection of future trajectories (2025-2100) using this calibrated model forced by CMIP6 climate scenarios (SSPs) coupled with spatially explicit socioeconomic scenarios (e.g., SSP-RCP narratives) defining management intensity.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-resolution, scenario-based projections of land cover succession for the Pyrenees, directly linking drought dynamics to ecological processes. It will provide a quantitative tool for assessing future landscape resilience and the trade-offs between carbon storage, biodiversity, and fire hazard under different climate and management futures.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of drought extremity metrics (SPEI) and socioeconomic scenario narratives into a spatially continuous model of vegetation succession, moving beyond simple climate-envelope models to capture process-driven, non-equilibrium dynamics in abandoned landscapes.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Impact of Woody Encroachment on Wildfire Susceptibility in the Spanish Pyrenees: A Spatiotemporal Analysis Integrating Land Cover Change and Drought Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Agricultural abandonment and subsequent woody encroachment is a widespread land cover change in Mediterranean mountain regions like the Pyrenees, altering fuel structure and continuity. This transformation occurs in a context of increasing drought frequency and intensity, a key focus of pre-2026 climate research. Understanding the compound effect of these processes is critical for proactive fire risk management in these ecologically and socio-economically valuable areas.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spatial pattern and rate of post-abandonment woodland expansion since the 1980s has significantly increased landscape-scale wildfire susceptibility in the Spanish Pyrenees, and that this effect is non-linearly amplified during drought episodes characterized by specific climatic water deficit thresholds.
proposals.method: We will employ a spatially continuous analysis using Landsat time series (1984-2025) to map annual land cover, specifically tracking the transition from agricultural/shrubland to continuous woodland using spectral indices and change detection algorithms (e.g., LandTrendr). This high-resolution land cover change chronology will be integrated with a gridded meteorological dataset to compute the Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI) at multiple timescales. Wildfire susceptibility will be modeled using a multi-temporal version of the Maximum Entropy (MaxEnt) algorithm, where annual fuel continuity layers (derived from land cover) and drought intensity (SPEI) serve as dynamic predictors, and historical fire perimeters (from EFFIS) are the response variable.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-resolution, spatially explicit quantification of how decades of woody encroachment have reconfigured fire-prone landscapes in the Pyrenees. It will advance the field of drought impacts by explicitly linking hydrological drought indices to a major socio-ecological risk mediated by land cover change, offering a transferable framework for other Mediterranean regions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, fine-scale spatiotemporal coupling of a continuous land cover change process (encroachment) with a key climatic driver (drought) within a dynamic fire susceptibility model, moving beyond static fuel maps or correlation-based studies prevalent pre-2026.

proposals.title: Spatio-temporal dynamics of ecosystem services in Mediterranean abandoned landscapes: A multi-decadal assessment of carbon, water, and erosion regulation
proposals.motivation: Agricultural abandonment is a widespread land-use change in Mediterranean Europe, driven by socio-economic shifts. This transformation fundamentally alters ecosystem structure and function, impacting critical services like carbon sequestration, water regulation, and erosion control, particularly in drought-prone areas. Quantifying these changes is essential for informing land management and climate adaptation policies in a region highly vulnerable to climate extremes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that post-abandonment landscape transformation leads to a non-linear, spatially heterogeneous recovery of ecosystem services, where carbon sequestration increases progressively, but water regulation services (e.g., runoff modulation) and erosion control show complex trajectories strongly mediated by vegetation succession patterns and the frequency of drought events.
proposals.method: We will employ a spatially continuous approach, integrating long-term satellite data (Landsat, MODIS) from 1985-2025. Vegetation phenology and biomass proxies (NDVI, EVI) will be used to derive carbon sequestration estimates. Water regulation will be assessed via the Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI) at fine scales and surface runoff models. Erosion control will be quantified using the Revised Universal Soil Loss Equation (RUSLE), where the cover-management factor (C) is dynamically derived from satellite time series. Trajectories of change will be analyzed for different abandonment ages and bioclimatic contexts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated, high-resolution quantification of multiple ecosystem service trajectories following abandonment across the Iberian Peninsula. It will directly link land-cover change dynamics to functional changes in carbon, water, and sediment cycles, offering a process-based understanding of ecosystem recovery. The findings will deliver actionable maps for prioritizing areas for natural regeneration or active restoration.
proposals.novelty: While abandonment studies often focus on land-cover change, this proposal uniquely integrates Vicente-Serrano's expertise in drought (SPEI) and phenology with a multi-service, process-based framework to unravel the complex and non-linear interactions between vegetation recovery, climate extremes, and ecosystem function over decades.

proposals.title: Quantifying structural landscape connectivity changes in the Pyrenees (1984-2025): A graph-theoretic assessment of drought-driven fragmentation
proposals.motivation: Mountain ecosystems like the Pyrenees are highly sensitive to climate change, with increasing drought frequency and intensity altering vegetation patterns and landscape structure. Pre-2026 studies, including those by Vicente-Serrano, have documented climatic drought trends and vegetation phenology changes, but a critical gap exists in quantifying how these processes translate into measurable changes in landscape connectivity and fragmentation, which are key for biodiversity and ecosystem resilience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the intensification of drought conditions over the last four decades has driven a significant increase in the structural fragmentation and a decrease in the functional connectivity of Pyrenean landscapes, with non-linear thresholds observable in specific ecotones.
proposals.method: We will apply landscape ecology metrics within a GIS framework to a time series of land cover maps (e.g., CORINE, or derived from Landsat and Sentinel-2). For each time slice, we will calculate graph-theoretic indices such as the Probability of Connectivity (PC) and the Equivalent Connected Area (ECA) for focal ecological classes (e.g., forests, alpine grasslands). We will correlate temporal changes in these connectivity metrics with a high-resolution drought index (Standardized Precipitation-Evapotranspiration Index, SPEI) time series, using spatial panel regression models to isolate the drought signal from other drivers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first spatially explicit, quantitative assessment of long-term structural connectivity change for the entire Pyrenean range. It will directly link drought metrics to landscape structural outcomes, offering a new framework for assessing ecosystem vulnerability and informing cross-border conservation strategies under climate change.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit application of landscape graph theory and connectivity metrics, common in ecology, to a long-term, macro-scale analysis of drought impacts in a mountain region, moving beyond standard land-cover change detection to assess functional landscape integrity.

proposals.title: The Paradox of Greening: Quantifying Trade-offs Between Increased Vegetation Density, Wildfire Hazard, and Water Yield in Mediterranean Drylands
proposals.motivation: In Mediterranean regions like Spain, climate change and land abandonment have driven widespread revegetation, often perceived as an unqualified ecological benefit. However, pre-2026 research indicates that increased vegetation density can elevate wildfire fuel loads and reduce water yield through higher evapotranspiration. A systematic, spatially explicit assessment of these trade-offs is critically needed to inform sustainable land and water management policies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in Mediterranean drylands, areas experiencing significant long-term revegetation (greening) will show a spatially correlated increase in wildfire hazard indices and a decrease in water yield proxies, challenging the uniform positive narrative of vegetation recovery.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-source remote sensing approach spanning 1985-2025. Using Landsat and MODIS time series, we will calculate long-term trends in vegetation indices (e.g., NDVI) and phenological metrics to map greening intensity. This will be integrated with a modified Drought Severity Index (DSI) incorporating evaporative demand. Wildfire hazard will be modeled using fuel accumulation proxies and fire weather indices (FWI). Water yield reduction will be estimated via a simplified Budyko-type water balance framework, using precipitation and actual evapotranspiration from satellite data. Spatial regression and change-point detection will analyze linkages between greening, drought, fire risk, and water yield.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-resolution, spatially continuous map of the revegetation-wildfire-water yield trade-off for the Iberian Peninsula. It will advance the conceptual framework for assessing ecological outcomes of land cover change by moving beyond a binary (good/bad) assessment. The results will deliver actionable science for regional fire management and water resource planning under changing climatic conditions.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have examined greening or drought impacts in isolation, the novelty lies in the integrated, spatially explicit quantification of the coupled negative externalities (fire risk and water loss) associated with a process (revegetation) typically viewed as positive, using a consistent methodology across decades.

proposals.title: Spatiotemporal dynamics of shrub encroachment and its impact on plant and animal biodiversity in abandoned Pyrenean slopes
proposals.motivation: Agricultural abandonment in European mountain regions like the Pyrenees triggers secondary succession, often leading to widespread shrub encroachment. This process fundamentally alters ecosystem structure and function, but its specific, spatially-explicit consequences for both plant and animal biodiversity remain poorly quantified. Understanding these biodiversity consequences is critical for predicting ecosystem resilience and informing conservation strategies in the context of global change and land-use transitions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that shrub encroachment following agricultural abandonment leads to a non-linear reorganization of biodiversity, initially increasing plant diversity but ultimately reducing beta-diversity and homogenizing habitats, with cascading negative effects on the diversity of animal taxa dependent on open or heterogeneous landscapes.
proposals.method: We will integrate multi-temporal Landsat and Sentinel-2 satellite imagery (1985-2025) to map shrub cover dynamics using a machine learning classifier (Random Forest) trained on field data. Biodiversity metrics (alpha and beta diversity) for plants and key animal groups (e.g., birds, orthopterans) will be derived from stratified field surveys across a chronosequence of abandonment. The relationship between shrub cover change trajectories (derived using the LandTrendr algorithm) and biodiversity metrics will be analyzed using spatial regression models and structural equation modeling to disentangle direct and indirect effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first spatially continuous, long-term assessment linking the process of shrub encroachment to multi-taxa biodiversity outcomes in Pyrenean landscapes. It will deliver a mechanistic understanding of how successional pathways influence biodiversity, moving beyond simple correlative studies. The findings will offer evidence-based guidance for managing abandoned lands to conserve biodiversity and ecosystem services.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the coupled spatiotemporal analysis of a key land-cover change process (shrub expansion) with concurrent, field-validated data for multiple biodiversity components, explicitly testing for cascading effects across trophic levels within a robust spatial-process framework.

proposals.title: Contrasting post-drought revegetation dynamics across European mountain ranges: A multi-decadal satellite-based assessment of the Alps, Carpathians, Pyrenees, and Scandinavian mountains
proposals.motivation: Mountain ecosystems are critical for biodiversity and water resources, yet they are highly vulnerable to climatic extremes, particularly drought. While studies like those in the Aragonese Pyrenees have documented local revegetation trends, a comprehensive, comparative analysis of how different European mountain systems recover from drought is lacking. This gap limits our ability to predict ecosystem resilience under future climate change and to inform regional adaptation strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that post-drought revegetation rates and trajectories differ significantly across major European mountain ranges due to distinct climatic regimes, land-use histories, and ecological communities. Specifically, we expect Mediterranean-influenced ranges (Pyrenees) to show more volatile recovery patterns than more continental (Carpathians) or boreal (Scandinavian) systems.
proposals.method: We will scale up the methodology from Vicente-Serrano's 2023 Spanish phenology study, applying a consistent spatial process analysis across all target ranges. Using the entire Landsat (1984-2025) and MODIS (2000-2025) archives, we will compute annual time series of the Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI) to identify drought years and the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) to assess vegetation response. Change detection algorithms (e.g., Breaks For Additive Seasonal and Trend - BFAST) will be used to analyze trends and breakpoints in vegetation productivity before and after major drought events, with results stratified by elevation, slope, and land cover.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first pan-European, spatially continuous comparison of mountain ecosystem resilience to drought, quantifying differential recovery capacities. It will directly contribute to understanding how large-scale bioclimatic gradients modulate the impacts of extreme events, offering a robust empirical basis for refining dynamic vegetation models used in regional climate assessments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic, continental-scale comparison of revegetation processes across contrasting mountain biomes using a harmonized, long-term satellite data framework, moving beyond single-range case studies to identify macro-ecological drivers of post-drought recovery.

proposals.title: Beyond NDVI: Assessing the Sufficiency of Vegetation Indices for Capturing Phenological Dynamics in Semi-Arid Mediterranean Ecosystems
proposals.motivation: In semi-arid Mediterranean ecosystems, sparse vegetation and high soil background exposure can significantly distort the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) signal, a primary tool for satellite-based phenology studies. This raises critical questions about the reliability of long-term NDVI-derived phenological trends for assessing vegetation response to climate change and drought. A systematic evaluation is needed to determine if NDVI alone is sufficient or if complementary indices are required for accurate ecological monitoring in these sensitive regions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that NDVI is insufficient for accurately characterizing key phenological metrics (e.g., start/end of season, peak productivity) in semi-arid Mediterranean landscapes due to the confounding influence of soil brightness and color, and that a multi-index approach incorporating soil-adjusted and chlorophyll-sensitive indices will provide a more robust representation of vegetation dynamics.
proposals.method: We will analyze a 30-year (1990-2020) Landsat and MODIS time series over representative semi-arid areas of Spain. For each pixel, we will compute NDVI alongside the Soil-Adjusted Vegetation Index (SAVI), the Enhanced Vegetation Index (EVI), and the Normalized Difference Water Index (NDWI). Phenological metrics will be extracted using a consistent change-point detection algorithm (e.g., Breaks For Additive Seasonal and Trend - BFAST). Statistical comparisons (correlation, RMSE, temporal trend analysis) of the metrics derived from different indices will be conducted, stratified by land cover and soil type.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for index selection in semi-arid phenology studies, directly informing best practices for monitoring drought impacts and vegetation change. It will contribute a critical, spatially explicit assessment of potential biases in long-term NDVI-based phenological records, enhancing the interpretation of ecological changes in Mediterranean climates.
proposals.novelty: While soil influence on NDVI is known, this study uniquely focuses on its systematic, long-term impact on derived *phenological metrics* (not just greenness values) across heterogeneous semi-arid landscapes, leveraging Dr. Vicente-Serrano's expertise in drought and spatial processes to bridge remote sensing methodology with applied climatology.

proposals.title: Disentangling the Drivers of Divergent Phenological Trends in Eurosiberian and Mediterranean Land Covers: A Multi-Scale Analysis of Climate Extremes and Biophysical Constraints
proposals.motivation: Recent satellite-based analyses reveal contrasting phenological trends across different land cover types in Spain, with significant implications for ecosystem services, carbon cycling, and water resources. Understanding the underlying drivers is critical, as these trends may reflect differential responses to climate change, particularly the increasing frequency and intensity of droughts and heatwaves. This is especially urgent in the Mediterranean region, a climate change hotspot, where shifts in vegetation dynamics can exacerbate land degradation and alter fire regimes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that divergent phenological trends between land cover types (e.g., forests, shrublands, croplands) are primarily driven by their contrasting sensitivities to climatic water deficits and temperature extremes, modulated by local biophysical conditions (e.g., soil properties, topography). The strength and sign of these drivers will differ systematically between the humid Eurosiberian and the water-limited Mediterranean regions.
proposals.method: We will employ a spatially continuous approach using long-term (1983-2025) satellite-derived phenological metrics (e.g., start/end of season, peak productivity) from Landsat and MODIS, harmonized with high-resolution land cover maps. For each land cover class, we will calculate annual Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI) time series at multiple timescales to capture drought impacts. We will then apply spatial panel data models and geographically weighted regression (GWR) to quantify the non-stationary relationship between phenological trends and key drivers: drought severity/ timing, heatwaves, antecedent conditions, soil water holding capacity, and topographic indices.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, spatially explicit understanding of how different ecosystems respond to climate stressors, moving beyond trend description to causal attribution. It will deliver high-resolution vulnerability maps identifying land covers and regions most at risk from phenological disruption. The findings will directly inform regional adaptation strategies for forestry, agriculture, and conservation under climate change.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have documented phenological trends, this proposal uniquely integrates Vicente-Serrano's expertise in drought index development (SPEI) and spatial processes to perform a driver-attribution analysis specifically tailored to the contrasting bioclimatic regions of Spain, linking extreme climate indices directly to land-cover-specific phenological responses at a fine spatial resolution.

proposals.title: Disentangling the impacts of agricultural intensification, irrigation, and land abandonment on long-term vegetation phenology trends in Spain (1983-2020)
proposals.motivation: Satellite-derived phenology trends are often attributed to climate change, but the distinct role of land management changes remains poorly quantified. In Mediterranean regions like Spain, concurrent processes of irrigation expansion, agricultural intensification, and land abandonment have dramatically altered landscapes. Understanding their specific contributions is crucial for accurate climate impact assessments and sustainable land-use planning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that long-term phenological trends (e.g., start/end of season, length) are primarily driven by localized land management changes rather than broad-scale climatic signals, with irrigation causing significant greening and advancement, and abandonment leading to delayed senescence.
proposals.method: We will use the existing 1983-2020 Landsat-derived phenology database for Spain. Phenological metrics will be extracted using TIMESAT algorithms. Statistical models (e.g., spatial panel regressions) will relate these metrics to high-resolution land-use change maps (CORINE, SIOSE), irrigation district data, and climatic covariates (SPEI drought index, temperature). The analysis will isolate management effects by comparing pixels with similar climate but different land-use trajectories.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first spatially explicit attribution of phenological trends to specific land management drivers in Spain. It will refine our understanding of anthropogenic forcing on ecosystem dynamics and improve the interpretation of satellite-observed greening/browning trends in the context of drought and climate change.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies correlate phenology with climate, this proposal explicitly quantifies the signal of land management by integrating long-term, high-resolution phenological data with detailed land-use change histories, moving beyond correlation to attribution.

proposals.title: Contrasting Pan-European Phenological Trends (1983-2024): A Spatially Continuous Assessment of Mediterranean and Continental Biomes Using a Fused NDVI Framework
proposals.motivation: Vegetation phenology is a key indicator of ecosystem responses to climate change, with significant implications for carbon cycling, water resources, and biodiversity. While regional studies like those in Spain exist, a consistent, spatially continuous analysis across Europe's diverse climatic zones is lacking, hindering a comprehensive understanding of biome-specific sensitivities. This gap limits our ability to predict ecosystem resilience under increasing drought extremes and climatic variability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the application of a harmonized, fused NDVI phenology framework will reveal divergent long-term trends and inter-annual variability between Mediterranean and Continental European biomes, with Mediterranean ecosystems showing stronger phenological shifts linked to drought severity and heatwaves.
proposals.method: We will scale up the validated methodology from Vicente-Serrano et al. (2025) for Spain to all of Europe. This involves fusing long-term NDVI data from AVHRR (LTDR v5) and MODIS (MOD13) to create a continuous, gap-filled 1-km resolution dataset from 1983-2024. Key phenological metrics (SOS, EOS, LOS) will be extracted using a modified TIMESAT algorithm. Trend analysis will employ non-parametric Mann-Kendall tests and Theil-Sen slopes, with spatial clustering to identify coherent regions. The relationship between phenological trends and climatic drivers (SPEI drought index, temperature) will be quantified using correlation and partial correlation analyses stratified by biome.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first spatially continuous, long-term phenological assessment for all of Europe using a consistent, fused-data framework, directly comparing Mediterranean and Continental systems. It will quantify the role of drought extremes versus warming in driving phenological change, offering critical empirical evidence for dynamic vegetation models and climate adaptation strategies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic, continental-scale application of a sophisticated data fusion and trend detection framework—previously applied only regionally—to explicitly test for biome-divergent phenological responses, moving beyond simple pan-European averages to reveal the spatial processes of change.

proposals.title: Inferring Agricultural Land Abandonment Histories in Mediterranean Drylands Through the Inverse Modeling of Erosion-Vegetation Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Land abandonment is a major driver of landscape change in Mediterranean Europe, with significant impacts on hydrology, erosion, and ecosystem services. However, the precise timing and sequence of abandonment are often poorly documented, hindering the understanding of long-term socio-ecological feedbacks. This project addresses this gap by developing a novel inverse modeling framework to reconstruct abandonment history from observable geomorphic and ecological signals.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatio-temporal patterns of soil erosion and vegetation recovery, quantified from remote sensing time series, encode a unique signature that can be inverted to infer the timing and type (gradual vs. abrupt) of historical agricultural land abandonment.
proposals.method: We will leverage long-term satellite data (Landsat, 1984-2025) to derive annual time series of vegetation indices (e.g., NDVI) and erosion proxies (e.g., bare soil indices, spectral indices sensitive to iron oxides). These will be combined with high-resolution topographic data (LiDAR-derived). The core inverse problem will be solved using a Bayesian hierarchical model. This model will integrate prior knowledge of abandonment drivers (e.g., slope, soil type, climate) with a process-based forward model simulating vegetation succession and erosion for different abandonment scenarios. Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling will be used to estimate the posterior probability distribution of abandonment timing and type for each pixel.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first spatially continuous, probabilistic map of land abandonment history for a study region in semi-arid Spain. It will advance the methodological integration of remote sensing phenology, geomorphology, and inverse modeling in land change science. The results will provide critical baseline data for assessing the legacy effects of abandonment on drought vulnerability and sediment yield.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies correlate abandonment with current erosion or vegetation states, this proposal inverts the causal chain to diagnose the historical driver from its contemporary effects. It uniquely combines Vicente-Serrano's expertise in drought and vegetation dynamics with an inverse problem approach common in geophysics but rarely applied to land use history.

proposals.title: Spatiotemporal Dynamics of Soil Erosion in Abandoned Farmlands Across the Mediterranean Basin: A Regional Synthesis Under Climate Extremes
proposals.motivation: Agricultural abandonment is a widespread land-use change across the Mediterranean, driven by socio-economic shifts and exacerbated by recurrent droughts. This process leaves soils vulnerable to degradation, yet the regional-scale patterns and drivers of post-abandonment erosion remain poorly quantified. Synthesizing this data is critical for assessing land degradation risks, carbon cycle impacts, and informing sustainable land management policies in a climate-sensitive region.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the magnitude and spatial patterns of soil erosion on abandoned Mediterranean farmlands are non-linear and primarily controlled by the interaction between the time since abandonment and the frequency/intensity of drought extremes, rather than by mean climatic conditions alone.
proposals.method: We will compile and harmonize a geodatabase of published and unpublished erosion rate measurements (e.g., from Cs-137, sediment traps, modeling studies) from abandoned fields, linked to spatial layers on abandonment timing and climate. Using a spatial analysis framework, we will apply advanced drought indices, specifically the Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI) at multiple timescales, to characterize drought conditions preceding and during erosion measurements. Statistical modeling (e.g., spatial regression, machine learning like Random Forest) will be used to disentangle the relative influence of drought extremes, land-cover succession, topography, and soil properties on observed erosion rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first pan-Mediterranean, data-driven synthesis of post-abandonment erosion, quantifying its regional heterogeneity. It will explicitly link erosion processes to climatic drought dynamics, advancing the integration of land-use change and climate extremes in desertification research. The resulting spatial risk framework will be a valuable tool for prioritizing restoration and conservation efforts.
proposals.novelty: While studies exist on local abandonment impacts, a basin-wide synthesis linking erosion to drought indices is lacking. The novel integration of spatially continuous drought characterizations (SPEI) with a compiled erosion database moves beyond case-study comparisons to identify coherent regional climatic drivers.

proposals.title: Multi-scale assessment of drought-driven vegetation vulnerability and erosion risk across European mountain systems
proposals.motivation: Mountain ecosystems are critical for biodiversity and water resources but are highly sensitive to climate extremes, particularly drought. Pre-2026 studies, including Vicente-Serrano's work in the Pyrenees, show that drought-induced vegetation degradation can accelerate soil erosion, yet the spatial patterns and drivers of this coupling across Europe's diverse mountain ranges remain poorly quantified. A continent-scale analysis is urgently needed to identify hotspots of vulnerability and inform regional adaptation strategies under increasing climatic stress.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the sensitivity of vegetation (NDVI) to drought, and its subsequent link to erosion risk, exhibits significant spatial heterogeneity across European mountain systems, driven by differences in climate regimes, topography, and land cover. Specifically, we expect Mediterranean mountain ranges (e.g., parts of the Alps) to show stronger coupling between drought stress and erosion potential than more humid ranges (e.g., Scandinavian mountains).
proposals.method: We will extend the Pyrenean methodological framework to the Alps, Carpathians, and Scandinavian mountains. Using long-term (1980s-2023) satellite data (e.g., Landsat, MODIS-derived NDVI) and high-resolution climate datasets (e.g., ERA5, E-OBS), we will compute the Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI) at multiple timescales. Statistical analyses (correlation, breakpoint detection, trend analysis) will quantify NDVI response to drought. Erosion risk will be assessed via the Revised Universal Soil Loss Equation (RUSLE), integrating drought-affected vegetation cover (C-factor) with topographic (SRTM) and soil data. Spatial regression (e.g., Geographically Weighted Regression) will identify regional controls.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce the first systematic, comparative map of drought-vegetation-erosion linkages across Europe's major mountain systems. It will quantify the differential vulnerability of these regions, providing a robust evidence base for prioritizing conservation and land management. The work will advance the spatial analysis of hydro-climatic extremes by explicitly linking atmospheric drought indices with biophysical land-surface processes at a continental scale.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have analyzed drought impacts or erosion in specific ranges, this is the first attempt to apply a consistent, process-based framework—integrating SPEI, NDVI phenology, and RUSLE—across all major European mountain systems, enabling a direct comparative assessment of their resilience and identifying non-linear, scale-dependent responses.

ideas.title: Apophatic Ecology: The Aesthetics of Climatic Negation and Vapor Pressure Deficit
ideas.description: This research applies the methods of negative theology to the study of forest die-off and climate-driven mortality. It posits that the lethal mechanism of vapor pressure deficit—an atmospheric 'thirst' defined by the negation of humidity—operates as an apophatic, negating force, stripping away the conditions for vitality. The resulting landscape of mortality (bleached forests, desiccated ecosystems) is thus analyzed as a secular aesthetic object, where meaning emerges not from what is present but from what has been catastrophically negated. This framework reframes ecological crisis as a form of metaphysical revelation, where the limits of habitability are made perceptible through the aesthetics of absence and loss.

ideas.title: Intra-annual characterology: Resolving the temporal anatomy of a literary persona's crisis
ideas.description: This research applies the principle of temporal downscaling from dendrochronology to literary character study. Just as dendrometer bands resolve a tree's growth response to drought at a weekly resolution, this approach dissects a single character's trajectory (e.g., Hamlet) at the resolution of scenes or significant interactions to map the precise timing, duration, and recovery of psychological 'stress events.' The synergy produces a 'xylogenesis of character,' where phases of paralysis, action, and rhetorical flourish are analyzed as growth rings, revealing the intra-annual anatomy of a crisis within the compressed timeframe of the narrative, thereby grounding the abstract 'absence-modernity' analysis in a concrete, temporal physiology.

ideas.title: Geocultural Basins of Modernity: A Pyrenean Framework for Pan-European Literary Analysis
ideas.description: This project proposes a geoculturally grounded, comparative framework for a Pan-European Modernity Survey. It applies the 'current-versus-potential' landscape analysis, developed for Pyrenean basins, as a replicable unit of study to major European literary regions (e.g., the Danube basin, the Baltic littoral, the Apennine valleys). By treating each region as a distinct cultural basin with its own dynamics of aesthetic modernization, the research moves beyond a simple continent-wide catalog to generate a systemic, spatialized understanding of how modernity emerges differentially, conditioned by local geographical, linguistic, and historical constraints versus transnational flows.

ideas.title: Human pressure peaked in nineteenth century

ideas.sourceTitle: Los efectos geoecológicos del cambio global en el Pirineo Central español: una revisión a distintas escalas espaciales y temporales

ideas.title: Phenomenology of Ecological Absence: Reading Forest Die-Off as Landscape Text
ideas.description: This interdisciplinary approach applies Husserlian phenomenological reduction to the interpretation of satellite-derived phenological anomalies in Mediterranean forests, treating zones of vegetation absence and disrupted seasonal cycles as meaningful 'silences' within a broader landscape text. Rather than reducing die-off to spectral index thresholds, the method systematically brackets prior ecological assumptions to let the spatial and temporal patterns of absence reveal their own significance—much as phenomenological literary analysis uncovers philosophical meaning in what characters do not say or do. The framework yields a dual contribution: it produces a hermeneutic layer atop remote sensing data that enriches ecological communication and policy narratives, while simultaneously grounding phenomenological methods in empirically observable, spatially explicit phenomena rather than purely textual ones. The result is a novel ecocritical remote sensing methodology that bridges environmental science and environmental humanities, enabling richer public and scholarly discourse on landscape-scale ecological decline.

ideas.title: The Cogito as a Dominant Paradigm: Recovering Suppressed Metaphysical Diversity
ideas.description: This research idea challenges the assumption that Descartes' *cogito* reveals the foundation of modernity by proposing it instead acts as a form of intellectual concealment, suppressing the premodern metaphysical richness it claimed to supersede. Drawing an analogy from ecology's solution to 'shifting baseline syndrome'—where historical data recovers lost biodiversity—the project adapts the methods of historical ecology to intellectual history. It treats the *cogito* as a dominant paradigm that reshaped the philosophical landscape, outcompeting alternative metaphysical frameworks. The adapted research program would systematically excavate and reconstruct pre-Cartesian metaphysical 'ecosystems' using textual paleontology, conceptual analysis, and the recovery of marginalized sources. The goal is to document the specific concepts, argumentative structures, and ontological commitments that were rendered obsolete or invisible, thereby challenging the narrative of the *cogito* as a necessary and revelatory advance and instead framing it as an event that imposed a new, narrower baseline for philosophical thought.

ideas.title: Multi-basin cellular xylem anatomy reveals landscape-scale hydraulic constraints on drought resilience
ideas.description: This research integrates quantitative wood anatomy with a multi-basin landscape comparison across the Pyrenees. By analyzing tracheid dimensions and wall thickness in trees from multiple basins with contrasting current versus potential forest cover, the study tests whether generalizable anatomical thresholds underpin landscape-scale drought resilience patterns. This approach moves beyond correlative landscape models to identify the specific, measurable xylem traits that either enable or limit forest persistence under different climatic and topographic regimes. The synthesis reveals how basin-scale environmental filters shape the functional anatomy of tree populations, providing a mechanistic bridge between cellular physiology and regional forest dynamics.

ideas.title: Comparative Philology of Ontic Givenness: Premodern Conceptual Ecologies and Modern Metacritique
ideas.description: This research systematically reconstructs premodern metaphysical frameworks from the ground up, avoiding the imposition of modern binaries. It begins with detailed philological and conceptual analysis of core ontological terms within specific textual traditions (e.g., Greco-Roman, Medieval, Indic, Sinic) to map their internal ecologies of 'givenness' or 'manifestness'. These distinct models are then brought into a comparative dialogue to articulate a spectrum of premodern ontological stances. Finally, this recovered complexity is used to metacritically interrogate the modern/postmodern focus on absence, testing its limits and historical specificity while refining a more nuanced transhistorical vocabulary.

ideas.title: The Pan-European Topography of Presence: Mapping the Dissolution of Premodern Ontology in Literary Modernity
ideas.description: This project inverts the standard 'absence-centric' study of literary modernity by first establishing a pan-European corpus-based model of how metaphysical presence (of the divine, cosmic order, or symbolic meaning) is formally constructed in premodern texts across major European literatures. It then uses this composite model of 'presence' as a diagnostic baseline to systematically survey and compare the timing, sequence, and formal mechanisms of its dissolution across different national and linguistic traditions from the late medieval period onward. The result is a large-scale, comparative topography of modernity's emergence, defined not by a single characteristic of absence but by the mapped recession of specific, pre-modern modes of presence, revealing distinct pathways and regional patterns in the continent's epistemological shift.

ideas.title: The Generative Concealment: Cartesian Cogito's Hidden Dimensions as Aesthetic Resource
ideas.description: This project systematically maps the dimensions of experience that the Cartesian cogito suppresses — embodiment, relationality, ecological embeddedness, the sacred — and then reinterprets each concealment not as a deficit but as a generative absence that opens distinctive aesthetic possibilities. Through comparative analysis of literary and philosophical texts from the seventeenth century to the present, it demonstrates how writers and artists have drawn creative energy precisely from the gaps the cogito produces, fashioning new forms of expression (the novel of interiority, the lyric of disenchantment, the aesthetics of the sublime) that would be impossible without those structured exclusions. The fusion yields a new theoretical concept — 'generative concealment' — which reframes modernity's metaphysical losses as conditions of possibility for its most characteristic cultural achievements, offering a counter-narrative to both nostalgic critiques of Cartesianism and triumphalist accounts of secular reason.

ideas.title: Cultural Canopy Loss: Mapping the Phenology of Literary and Ecological Absence Across Modernizing Landscapes
ideas.description: This project develops a cross-domain methodology that models the disappearance of literary forms, motifs, and narrative traditions in non-Western literatures as a phenological process analogous to forest die-off detectable via remote sensing. Just as satellite-derived vegetation indices track canopy health over large spatial extents and flag anomalous decline, computational text analysis pipelines would track the 'health' of literary features (genre diversity, thematic richness, stylistic complexity) across corpora spanning decades of modernization in regions such as the Arabic nahda, Japanese Meiji-era literature, or West African postcolonial writing. Temporal anomaly detection algorithms adapted from phenological remote sensing—such as breakpoint analysis and seasonal trend decomposition—would be applied to publication metadata and textual feature time series to identify critical transition points where traditional literary ecosystems undergo rapid collapse or transformation. The result is a scalable, evidence-based framework for diagnosing cultural absence at regional scales, bridging environmental science methodology with digital humanities to reveal how modernization produces parallel patterns of loss in both ecological and cultural landscapes.

ideas.title: Absence as Productive Rather Than Deficient

ideas.title: Pan-European Modernity Survey via Phylogenetic Mapping
ideas.description: This research adapts the phylogenetic mapping solution from evolutionary biology to the problem of scaling up a modernity survey across all major European literatures. Just as biologists use conserved genetic markers to build trees tracing trait evolution across species, this project will identify a core set of homologous and analogous aesthetic markers of modernity across different literary traditions. By applying computational phylogenetic models, the research will map the pathways of aesthetic emergence—distinguishing between diffusion from common sources, horizontal cultural transfer, and convergent independent evolution—thereby creating a dynamic, continent-wide model of modernism's development that accounts for both deep structural commonalities and unique regional innovations.

ideas.title: Negative Archaeology: Absence as an Operative Constraint in Post-Metaphysical Aesthetics
ideas.description: This research systematically reframes the absence of premodern metaphysics not as a lack but as a set of operative constraints that actively shape contemporary aesthetic production. It develops a method of 'negative archaeology' to catalog how specific metaphysical absences (e.g., of cosmic order, teleology, or symbolic correspondence) generate distinct formal problems, which artists solve through techniques like non-linear narrative, material indeterminacy, or ironic citation. The outcome is a robust diagnostic framework that moves beyond celebration or lament to explain the causal link between historical-philosophical loss and the emergence of specific aesthetic forms and genres after modernism.

ideas.title: Narrative Survival: Modeling Forest Mortality as Ecological Drama through Character-Driven Hazard Analysis
ideas.description: This research develops a dual-framework that applies Cox proportional hazards and competing-risks models not only to drought-stressed tree mortality data but also to the temporal trajectories of literary characters facing existential threats, treating both as subjects whose 'survival' unfolds under compounding stressors. Individual trees are reconceived as characters with life-narratives shaped by site history, species identity, and cumulative stress exposure, enabling the construction of 'ecological dramatis personae' that make complex hazard model outputs legible to non-specialist audiences through narrative archetypes (the resilient elder, the vulnerable pioneer, the slow decliner). Simultaneously, the framework applies survival analysis formalisms to literary corpora, modeling how authors like Dostoevsky or Camus construct characters whose philosophical mortality mirrors actuarial risk curves, revealing that narrative tension often follows competing-risks structures. The fusion yields a new interdisciplinary methodology—narrative epidemiology—where quantitative survival modeling and humanistic character analysis mutually enrich each other, improving both public understanding of ecological crisis and literary-theoretical accounts of how subjectivity is constructed under temporal threat.

ideas.title: The Sapwood Self: Literary Characters as Hydraulic Proxies in Modernity
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the philosophical-literary analysis of character with the ecological model of deceptive vitality. It posits that key characters in modern and postmodern literature can be read as philosophical arguments about a specific form of subjectivity: one that maintains the outward production of a coherent self (the 'sapwood' of personality, dialogue, and action) by drawing on cultural or psychological reserves, while already undergoing an irreversible collapse in the core 'hydraulic' systems that sustain authentic connection, belief, or meaning. The framework challenges readings that take a character's apparent narrative vigour at face value, instead diagnosing a latent modernity where the self functions as a convincing but doomed proxy for a prior, integral state of being.

ideas.title: A Pre-Registered, Causal Framework for Testing Generalizable Drivers of Landscape Change Across Pyrenean Basins
ideas.description: This study moves beyond simple replication by implementing a pre-registered, hypothesis-driven sampling and analysis design to rigorously test the generalizability of landscape change drivers. A stratified random sample of Pyrenean basins is selected based on climatic, geological, and anthropogenic gradients. For each basin, potential natural vegetation and hydromorphological states are modeled using a standardized, algorithmically applied procedure that removes subjective judgment. The analysis then employs a causal inference framework to quantify and compare the relative influence of pre-specified drivers (e.g., historical land use, climate aridity, slope) on the observed divergence from potential states across the entire gradient, producing robust, generalizable conclusions about landscape dynamics in the region.

ideas.title: Literary Characters as Philosophical Arguments: A Dynamical Systems Approach
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'individual-based model' (IBM) solution from theoretical ecology to challenge the boundary between literary characters and philosophical arguments. In ecology, IBMs solved the problem of reducing complex populations to homogeneous averages by instead simulating unique individuals with specific traits and interaction rules, from which system-level dynamics emerge. Adapted here, a literary character is analyzed as a philosophical IBM: a set of core propositional rules about selfhood, agency, and relation to the world. By 'running' this character-model through its narrative interactions and constraints, the research demonstrates how the character's lived trajectory within the story constitutes a rigorous, testable argument about modern subjectivity. The philosophical claim is thus found not merely in what the character says, but in the dynamical consequences of their operational 'code' within the fictional system, offering a formal bridge between literary embodiment and propositional logic.

ideas.title: Single-Character Deep Phenotyping for Absence-Modernity Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the 'deep phenotyping' approach from genetics and precision medicine to the literary research problem of analyzing modernity through the lens of absence. Just as geneticists intensively study a single model organism with multiple analytical layers to understand a complex disease, this research will perform a multi-layered 'deep phenotyping' of a single literary character (Don Quixote or Hamlet). The analogous solution involves exhaustive data collection from the text—every utterance, action, and description—combined with analysis of linguistic patterns, narrative function, historical critical reception, and philosophical interpretations. By building this comprehensive profile, the research aims to establish a definitive, granular model of how the character embodies the crisis of modernity and the phenomenology of absence, turning the scaled-down single-character study into a robust, transferable analytical framework.

ideas.title: The Epistemic Closure of the Cogito: A Genealogy of Excluded Premodern Metaphysics
ideas.description: This research project moves beyond a general critique to provide a historically grounded genealogy of epistemic closure. It identifies specific metaphysical concepts from defined premodern traditions (e.g., participatory being, analogical predication, intellectual illumination) and meticulously traces their fate in the early modern period. By analyzing the argumentative strategies, foundational definitions, and educational reforms initiated by Descartes and his influential followers, the project demonstrates how the cogito operated not merely as a new foundation, but as an engine of conceptual amnesia, actively constructing the boundaries of what could thereafter be considered rationally admissible.

ideas.title: Linear climate-vegetation-erosion causal chain assumption

ideas.title: Phenomenological Bracketing of Cumulative Stress in Narrative Die-Offs
ideas.description: This research applies the Husserlian method of phenomenological reduction (bracketing) to literary and narrative analysis, but directs it toward the problem of die-off or systemic collapse. Instead of seeking a single dramatic catalyst for a character's philosophical death or a society's fall, the method systematically suspends that assumption to attend to the layered, multi-year 'stressors' within the narrative field: accumulated silences, eroded relationships, normalized deprivations, and repeated minor traumas that are often backgrounded. By bracketing the protagonist's final crisis, the analysis reveals how the cumulative phenomenological absences—what is persistently missing or degraded over time—create the necessary and sufficient conditions for die-off, offering a more nuanced model of narrative causality rooted in attrition rather than event.

ideas.title: Scale down to individual slope analysis

ideas.title: The Argument of Absence: Literary Characters as Propositions of a Post-Metaphysical Subject
ideas.description: This research direction synthesizes the two ideas by arguing that the defining formal and existential features of key modern literary characters—their fragmentation, alienation, or ontological uncertainty—are not merely illustrations of philosophical problems but are themselves sophisticated arguments. These character-arguments become legible and necessary only in the absence of a coherent metaphysical backdrop (God, cosmic order, essential self). The project would analyze how authors like Kafka, Woolf, or Beckett construct characters whose very form—their narrative gaps, paradoxical consciousness, or linguistic instability—propositions a new model of subjectivity that is generative precisely because it emerges from and is shaped by this foundational absence, producing a distinct aesthetics of the modern.

ideas.title: Lens, Foil, and Principle: A Structured Single-Character Framework for Theorizing Absence
ideas.description: This approach retains the intensive focus on a single core character (e.g., Hamlet) as the primary lens for analyzing a specific modality of absence. However, it systematically strengthens the analysis by first contrasting this lens with its immediate textual foil (e.g., Horatio or Fortinbras) to isolate the defining traits of the absence. It then tests the resulting conceptual framework against a deliberately chosen counter-example from a different literary period (e.g., the protagonist of Voltaire's 'Candide') to challenge and refine its boundaries. The final output is an explicitly articulated theoretical principle about 'absence-modernity' that is grounded in deep character analysis but demonstrated to have broader explanatory power.

ideas.title: Literary Characters as Counter-Cogito: Unconcealing Suppressed Subjectivities
ideas.description: This research direction posits that key literary characters from the early modern period onward function as embodied philosophical counter-arguments to Descartes' cogito. By examining characters whose subjectivity is constituted through relationality, embodiment, passion, or non-rational modes of being, we can trace an alternative genealogy of the modern self that the cogito's formulation actively concealed. These characters do not merely illustrate philosophical ideas; they enact and preserve forms of subjective experience—such as tragic fate, chivalric honor, or mystical union—that were marginalized by the cogito's epistemological turn. Thus, the literary becomes the primary archive for accessing the metaphysical richness that foundational philosophical modernity declared obsolete.

ideas.title: Symmetry-Breaking in Aesthetics: Absence as a Generative Constraint
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption that the loss of premodern metaphysics is a negative deficit for aesthetics. By drawing an analogy from theoretical physics and cosmology—specifically the concept of 'symmetry breaking' where the transition from a symmetric to an asymmetric state generates cosmic structure—we reconceptualize absence as a generative constraint. The analogous solution shows how a broken, less-ordered state is productive, not deficient. Adapted to aesthetics, the historical absence of a unifying metaphysical framework is seen as a symmetry-breaking event. This break forces art and interpretation into new, asymmetric conditions, actively generating the diverse forms, radical interpretations, and self-reflexive modes that characterize modern and contemporary aesthetics. The research would investigate how specific movements and works are not merely responses to loss but are structurally determined and made possible by this foundational absence.

ideas.title: Simulating transient vegetation trajectories under climate change with dynamic global vegetation models
ideas.description: This research moves beyond equilibrium-based potential vegetation maps by using a process-based Dynamic Global Vegetation Model (DGVM) forced with climate projections to simulate the transient and lagged responses of plant functional types. The system models key non-equilibrium processes, including species dispersal limits, establishment, mortality, disturbance, and soil-vegetation feedbacks, to project the rate and pathway of landscape change. Outputs are probabilistic maps showing vegetation states and their likelihood at decadal intervals, along with metrics of disequilibrium, providing a dynamic assessment of how and when warming alters expected landscapes without human activity.

ideas.title: Linking xylem anatomical plasticity to non-structural carbohydrate depletion thresholds across drought cycles
ideas.description: This fused research direction simultaneously monitors seasonal non-structural carbohydrate (NSC) concentrations and quantitative tracheid anatomy (lumen diameter, cell-wall thickness, cell density) across multiple drought cycles in conifer species. By pairing high-resolution wood microcore sampling with tissue-level sugar and starch assays at matched time points, the study can quantify how carbon allocation to xylem reinforcement during drought depletes stored NSC reserves, and whether trees that produce thicker-walled, narrower-lumen tracheids under water stress reach critical carbohydrate depletion thresholds faster than those with more conservative anatomical responses. The approach enables construction of a coupled carbon-hydraulic budget model that predicts mortality risk from the interaction between anatomical investment costs and reserve dynamics, rather than from either variable in isolation. Ultimately, it identifies species- and site-specific anatomical strategies that maximize hydraulic safety per unit carbon spent, offering a mechanistic framework for forecasting drought-induced tree death.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: predict abandonment date from NDVI

ideas.title: Confounder-stratified sentinel network for causal attribution of pan-Mediterranean conifer die-off
ideas.description: This study designs a stratified sentinel-site network across the Mediterranean basin, where sites are selected and paired using pre-existing data on soil properties, fire history, bark beetle outbreaks, and forest management to isolate drought-driven mortality from confounding agents. At each sentinel site, ground-validated tree mortality inventories are linked to multi-temporal remote sensing spectral trajectories, enabling a causal attribution framework that distinguishes drought die-off from pest, fire, and land-use-driven canopy loss. The network spans diverse conifer species and climatic gradients but maintains interpretability through matched-pair and propensity-score designs rather than naive spatial extrapolation. This approach yields ecologically actionable die-off maps with quantified attribution confidence, directly supporting Mediterranean forest adaptation policy.

ideas.title: Temporally Explicit Phenological Trajectory Modeling for Die-off Forecasting
ideas.description: This research fuses temporal landscape transition modeling with remote sensing phenology to develop a dynamic framework for forecasting forest die-off. Instead of detecting anomalies from a static baseline, it models the stepwise, phenological trajectory of vegetation indices (e.g., NDVI, EVI) over time, simulating how normal seasonal patterns degrade under stress. This allows for the identification of critical transition phases and tipping points in the die-off process, providing early warning of landscape-scale mortality. The approach enables probabilistic forecasting of die-off risk by simulating future phenological trajectories under different climate and disturbance scenarios.

ideas.description: Challenge the boundary separating literary characters from philosophical propositions about subjectivity and modernity.
ideas.sourceTitle: LA EVOLUCIÓN DE LOS ESTUDIOS SOBRE SEQUÍAS CLIMÁTICAS EN ESPAÑA EN LAS ÚLTIMAS DÉCADAS

ideas.title: Quantifying the Absent Subject: Digital Analysis of Character as Negative Philosophical Argument
ideas.description: This project applies computational text analysis (distant reading, semantic vector modeling, and pattern detection for negation and omission) to early modern literary corpora to systematically identify and analyze characters defined primarily by absence, elision, or radical incompleteness. It posits that such characters are not merely aesthetic figures but function as negative philosophical arguments—embodied critiques of coherent subjectivity, agency, or knowability. By quantifying the rhetorical and narrative patterns that produce these 'absent subjects,' the research bridges digital humanities with the history of philosophy, offering a data-driven framework for understanding how literature prefigures modern philosophical concerns about the void at the center of the self.

ideas.title: Coral reef bleaching network approach applied to pan-Mediterranean conifer die-off mapping
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from marine ecology, where the Global Coral Reef Monitoring Network successfully scaled local coral bleaching assessments to a pan-tropical surveillance system by combining standardized in-situ survey protocols with satellite-derived thermal stress products. In that system, hundreds of independent reef sites report bleaching severity using common metrics, which are then integrated with NOAA Coral Reef Watch Degree Heating Weeks to produce regional early warnings and identify thermal refugia. Adapting this approach, we propose a pan-Mediterranean Conifer Die-off Watch that federates ground-based forest health monitoring plots across the basin—from Iberian P. halepensis woodlands to Balkan P. nigra stands and Alpine-margin Abies alba forests—under a standardized protocol for scoring crown transparency, needle loss, bark beetle damage, and tree mortality. Satellite-derived drought indicators (Standardized Precipitation-Evapotranspiration Index, MODIS evaporative stress index, Sentinel-2 chlorophyll red-edge indices) serve the same role as sea surface temperature anomalies in the coral system, providing continuous spatial coverage between plot visits. By unifying plot-level mortality records with gridded climate stress layers in a shared database, the network would enable cross-species, cross-climate comparisons of drought mortality thresholds, reveal spatial refugia where die-off risk is lower than expected, and produce basin-wide early warning maps analogous to coral bleaching alerts. The analogy is particularly apt because both systems involve sessile organisms experiencing threshold-driven mortality from climatic extremes, both require bridging sparse in-situ observations with wall-to-wall remote sensing, and both benefit from multi-national coordination to achieve the spatial coverage needed for macro-ecological inference.
