ideas.title: Pluralizing Literary Negation: A Co-Constructive Taxonomy of Absence Across World Literatures
ideas.description: This project replaces the unidirectional export of a Western absence framework with a collaborative, multi-tradition research design in which scholars from at least four distinct literary traditions (e.g., classical Arabic adab, Chinese wenyan fiction, Yoruba orature, and Sanskrit kāvya) independently theorize their tradition's native concepts of literary withholding, silence, and negation. A structured comparative protocol then maps these local frameworks against each other and against the original Shakespeare-Cervantes model, identifying genuine structural homologies, productive tensions, and incommensurable differences. The result is a pluralized taxonomy of literary negation that resists false universalism while enabling rigorous cross-cultural comparison. This adversarial redesign transforms the project from a potentially reductive application exercise into a genuinely dialogic contribution to world-literary theory.

ideas.title: Phylogenetic Tracing of Aesthetic Absence: Adapting Molecular Phylogenetics to Map the Transmission of Negative Aesthetics from Descartes to Phenomenology
ideas.description: This research adapts molecular phylogenetic methods from evolutionary biology to systematically trace the intellectual lineage of absence-based aesthetics across post-Cartesian philosophy. In biology, phylogenetic inference reconstructs evolutionary trees by comparing homologous traits across species, modeling rates of change, and inferring ancestral states at internal nodes. Analogously, this project treats philosophical texts as taxa and specific aesthetic concepts related to absence (negation, void, silence, the sublime withdrawal, das Nichts) as heritable characters that undergo 'mutation' as they pass from one thinker to another. Using a corpus of canonical texts from Descartes, Pascal, the German Romantics (Novalis, Schelling), Schopenhauer, Heidegger, Merleau-Ponty, and Sartre, the study would: (1) operationalize aesthetic-of-absence concepts as discrete or continuous character states through computational text analysis and expert coding; (2) construct a conceptual phylogeny using cladistic or network-based methods to map how these ideas branched, converged, or were independently reinvented; (3) apply ancestral state reconstruction to infer the probable conceptual content at key transitional nodes (e.g., the late-Enlightenment junction between rationalist and Romantic aesthetics); and (4) identify 'horizontal transfer' events—cases where absence aesthetics jumped between otherwise unrelated philosophical traditions, analogous to lateral gene transfer in prokaryotes. This framework moves beyond traditional intellectual history's narrative approach by providing a formal, reproducible model of conceptual evolution that can be tested, refined, and compared across different historiographic hypotheses.

ideas.title: Dark Matter Mapping for the Cartesian Cogito: Adapting Astrophysical Inference of Hidden Mass to Catalog Concealed Dimensions of Modernity
ideas.description: This project adapts the methodology of astrophysical dark matter detection to the history of philosophy. In astrophysics, invisible mass is cataloged not by direct observation but by measuring anomalies in the behavior of visible matter — galaxy rotation curves that refuse to match predictions, light bent where no visible source exists. Analogously, the Cartesian cogito functions as the 'visible matter' of modern subjectivity: it is the explicit, canonical foundation. Yet literary and philosophical texts from Montaigne to Dostoevsky to Fanon exhibit persistent 'anomalies' — accounts of selfhood that deviate from what pure Cartesian subjectivity would predict. The method proceeds in three steps: (1) construct a baseline model of what philosophical discourse about the self should look like if the cogito were the complete account (the 'no-dark-matter' prediction); (2) perform systematic literary-philosophical comparison across periods and traditions to identify every deviation — affective excess, embodied resistance, colonial erasure, gendered silence; (3) catalog these deviations as a 'dark matter map' of what the cogito conceals about modernity. Each hidden dimension is characterized by its 'gravitational signature': the specific type of distortion it produces in visible philosophical discourse. This approach transforms an interpretive humanistic question into a quasi-systematic detection problem, offering a replicable framework for mapping the latent structures that canonical philosophical concepts suppress.

ideas.title: Cumulative stress loading framework for forest die-off: Lessons from material fatigue science
ideas.description: This idea challenges the prevailing assumption that a single extreme drought event is the proximate cause of forest die-off by drawing an analogy to fatigue failure in materials science. In structural engineering, components rarely fail from a single load application; instead, repeated sub-critical stress cycles progressively accumulate micro-damage (micro-cracks, dislocation pile-ups) until a critical fatigue threshold is reached and catastrophic fracture occurs. Miner's cumulative damage rule formalizes this by summing the ratio of applied cycles to failure cycles at each stress level—when the sum reaches 1.0, failure is predicted. Adapting this framework to forest ecology, each drought year (or dry season) would contribute a fractional damage dose to trees based on the severity and duration of hydraulic stress relative to species-specific xylem vulnerability curves and carbon starvation thresholds. A cumulative stress damage index would be computed across multiple years, incorporating recovery periods (analogous to stress-relief annealing in metals) that partially reverse accumulated damage. Die-off would be predicted not by any single drought exceeding a lethal threshold, but by the cumulative index crossing a critical value—explaining why some severe droughts cause no die-off (low prior damage) while moderate droughts sometimes trigger widespread mortality (high accumulated prior damage). The research would involve reconstructing multi-decadal drought stress histories at known die-off sites, calibrating species-specific damage accumulation and recovery functions, and testing whether a cumulative damage model outperforms single-event drought severity in predicting observed die-off patterns across biomes.

ideas.title: Temporally-resolved landscape recovery trajectories: modeling post-drought resilience as a dynamic transition process
ideas.description: This research models post-drought forest recovery as a multi-stage temporal transition process across landscapes, tracking how surviving trees progress through discrete physiological recovery phases (hydraulic restoration, carbon reserve replenishment, canopy rebuilding) rather than treating recovery as a single endpoint. By adapting landscape transition dynamics frameworks to simulate spatially-explicit recovery trajectories, the approach reveals how local site conditions, species trait portfolios, and neighborhood interactions accelerate or stall transitions between recovery stages. The temporal simulation identifies critical bottleneck phases where trees are most vulnerable to secondary mortality, enabling prediction of which landscape patches will achieve full recovery versus those trapped in degraded stable states. This fusion yields a dynamic resilience mapping tool that connects individual tree physiology to landscape-scale recovery forecasting under projected drought regimes.

ideas.title: Computational-Historical Verification of Aesthetic Absence Transmission from Post-Cartesian to Romantic and Phenomenological Thought
ideas.description: This study develops a dual-verification framework for testing whether the aesthetics of absence in post-Cartesian philosophy constituted a genuine intellectual lineage into Romantic and phenomenological traditions, or merely a parallel emergence. It combines computational corpus analysis—concept co-occurrence networks and stylometric fingerprinting across primary texts from Descartes through Hegel, Schelling, and Heidegger—with traditional historiographic evidence of citation chains, correspondence, and documented reading practices. Claims of influence are accepted only when both computational textual convergence and material transmission evidence align, providing explicit falsification criteria that guard against the confirmation bias endemic to conventional intellectual history. The framework produces a weighted transmission graph distinguishing strong, weak, and spurious influence pathways, offering a replicable methodology applicable to other cross-epoch intellectual genealogies.

ideas.title: Disentangling cumulative hydraulic depletion from acute drought shock as drivers of forest die-off via factorial manipulation and causal inference
ideas.description: Forest die-off events are typically attributed to extreme single-year droughts, yet these events are confounded with preceding multi-year stress, making causal attribution ambiguous from observational data alone. This study combines replicated rainout-shelter experiments—imposing factorial chronic-moderate versus acute-severe water deficits of matched cumulative magnitude—with continental-scale distributed lag non-linear models and structural causal graphs applied to satellite and climate reanalysis data. Mechanistic monitoring of stored carbohydrates, xylem hydraulic failure, and progressive canopy loss within experimental cohorts provides process-level evidence for which stress pathway dominates mortality thresholds. By triangulating manipulative and observational causal inference approaches, the design overcomes the core confound between cumulative depletion and peak intensity, yielding actionable predictions of die-off risk under climate scenarios that differ in drought duration versus severity.

ideas.title: Linking cambial dynamics to hydraulic function: a temporally resolved wood anatomy framework for drought response attribution
ideas.description: This approach integrates micro-core time series sampling of cambial activity with quantitative tracheid anatomical measurements and hydraulic vulnerability curves across experimentally imposed drought treatments. By tracking cell differentiation timing through repeated cambial biopsies during the growing season, the system identifies precisely when drought signals alter tracheid dimensions and wall thickness, resolving the confound between developmental constraints and plastic acclimation. Pairing anatomical traits with percent loss of conductivity measurements directly links cellular-level structural variation to whole-organ hydraulic function, providing mechanistic evidence rather than correlational anatomy. The experimental design includes well-watered controls, moderate, and severe drought treatments replicated across multiple genotypes to partition genetic versus environmental contributions to anatomical drought responses.

ideas.title: Borrowing from materials science microstructural analysis to decode xylem drought responses
ideas.description: This idea transfers the analytical toolkit of cellular solids mechanics from materials science to plant xylem hydraulics. In metallurgy and materials engineering, the mechanical performance of porous and cellular materials (metallic foams, honeycomb structures) is predicted by quantifying microstructural parameters: cell size distributions via stereological sampling, wall thickness measured through image analysis of cross-sections, and void connectivity mapped through serial sectioning or micro-CT. Crucially, materials scientists have learned that population-level statistics—particularly the tail of the size distribution modeled by Weibull functions—govern catastrophic failure far better than mean values alone. We adapt this by applying identical quantitative stereological protocols to xylem cross-sections across species and drought intensities, measuring not just mean tracheid diameter and wall thickness but their full statistical distributions, spatial autocorrelation, and connectivity topology through pit membranes. Weibull survival analysis, originally developed to predict brittle fracture in ceramics and fatigue failure in metals, is repurposed to model the probability of air-seeding events as a function of tracheid geometric parameters. Finite element models of cellular solid deformation under load are recast as hydraulic network models where negative xylem pressure replaces mechanical stress, cell wall implosion replaces buckling, and air-seeding through pit membranes replaces crack propagation. This cross-disciplinary transfer provides a mechanistic, physics-based framework for linking cellular-scale wood anatomy to whole-organ hydraulic vulnerability, moving beyond empirical correlations toward predictive models of drought-induced xylem dysfunction.

ideas.title: Borrowing from cardiac rehabilitation science to model post-drought recovery trajectories in trees
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy between post-drought tree recovery and post-myocardial infarction cardiac rehabilitation. In medicine, recovery after a major cardiac event is not studied as a single outcome but as a trajectory: patients are monitored longitudinally for functional markers (cardiac output, exercise tolerance, inflammatory biomarker clearance), and latent class growth models are used to identify distinct recovery phenotypes—rapid recoverers, slow but complete recoverers, and those with permanently reduced function. Pre-event fitness, comorbidities, and the timing of therapeutic intervention are the key predictors of trajectory class membership. Adapting this to tree ecophysiology, we propose monitoring post-drought recovery trajectories across multiple physiological axes: hydraulic conductivity (analogous to cardiac output restoration), non-structural carbohydrate reserves (analogous to metabolic reserves), cambial activity (analogous to tissue repair rate), and canopy leaf area index (analogous to functional capacity). Using repeated measurements over one to three growing seasons following severe drought, latent trajectory modeling would classify surviving trees into recovery phenotypes. Pre-drought anatomical and physiological traits—sapwood capacitance, wood density, xylem vulnerability curves, stored starch concentrations, and rooting depth—serve as baseline predictors, paralleling pre-event fitness metrics in cardiology. Early post-drought signals such as the rate of embolism refilling in the first weeks after rewetting would function as triage biomarkers. This dual-scaling approach operates across biological scales (cell-level hydraulic repair to whole-tree canopy recovery) and across disciplinary boundaries (medical rehabilitation science to forest ecophysiology), providing a structured, clinically inspired framework for understanding why some trees bounce back from drought while others languish or succumb to secondary stressors.

ideas.title: Compensated decline: Borrowing from oncology's biomarker-function dissociation to reassess tree vigour metrics
ideas.description: This idea draws a direct analogy between tree sapwood production as a vigour proxy and tumour volume as a cancer progression proxy. In oncology, the reliance on tumour size via RECIST imaging criteria led to dangerous misclassifications: tumours that appeared stable were in fact developing resistance and metastatic capacity. The field resolved this by adopting functional biomarkers (ctDNA, metabolic PET, liquid biopsies) that track the biological behaviour of the disease rather than its structural footprint. Similarly, trees may sustain sapwood production by mobilising stored carbohydrate reserves even as their hydraulic transport system undergoes irreversible embolism and dysfunction. Sapwood area, like tumour volume, is a lagging structural indicator that can mask functional collapse. The adapted research programme proposes: (1) defining a 'compensated decline' phenotype in trees, analogous to pseudo-progression in oncology, where structural metrics remain normal but functional capacity is degrading; (2) developing a multi-modal vigour panel combining sapwood area with hydraulic conductivity measurements (percent loss of conductivity via bench dehydration), acoustic emission sensors for real-time cavitation detection, and non-structural carbohydrate assays to quantify reserve depletion; and (3) validating this panel in drought-stressed forest stands to determine the lead time between functional decline detection and eventual structural collapse (growth cessation or mortality). This framework would fundamentally shift forest health monitoring from retrospective structural assessment to prospective functional diagnosis.

ideas.title: Disentangling direct thermal damage from VPD-mediated hydraulic failure in vegetation die-off using causal mediation analysis
ideas.description: This study employs a causal mediation framework to separate the direct cellular thermal damage pathway from the indirect vapor pressure deficit (VPD)-mediated hydraulic failure pathway in driving vegetation mortality during extreme heat events. By identifying natural experiments—humid heatwaves where temperature is extreme but VPD remains moderate, and dry warm periods where VPD is high but temperatures are sub-lethal—the approach breaks the collinearity that confounds purely observational analyses. The framework is validated against factorial growth-chamber experiments crossing temperature and humidity treatments, with mortality monitored alongside physiological markers of thermal damage (membrane leakage, chlorophyll fluorescence) and hydraulic stress (xylem embolism, stomatal conductance). This design directly addresses the fundamental confound between temperature and moisture availability, yielding mechanistically interpretable and policy-relevant thresholds for die-off risk under future climate scenarios.

ideas.title: Multi-sensor phenological trajectory divergence for disambiguating Mediterranean forest die-off from reversible drought stress
ideas.description: This study develops a multi-sensor early warning system for Mediterranean forest die-off that overcomes the critical inability of standard vegetation indices to distinguish irreversible mortality from reversible drought stress. By fusing Sentinel-2 optical phenological trajectories, Sentinel-1 SAR canopy water content dynamics, and land surface temperature anomalies into a multi-dimensional feature space, the approach tracks trajectory divergence between stressed-but-recovering and lethally-declining forest stands across consecutive growing seasons. A random forest classifier trained on field-validated die-off and survival plots learns the spectral-structural-thermal divergence signatures unique to true mortality, enabling landscape-scale mapping with quantified false positive rates. The system is validated across three major Mediterranean forest types using existing die-off inventory networks, providing both retrospective attribution and near-real-time early warning capability.

ideas.title: Formal Argument Mapping of Narrative Subjectivity: A Computational-Philosophical Framework
ideas.description: This research develops a formal methodology to reconstruct literary characters as testable philosophical models of subjectivity. It employs argument-mapping techniques and computational narrative analysis to extract the implicit logical structure of a character's worldview, decisions, and interactions, treating the narrative as a thought experiment. The framework then subjects these reconstructed 'character-arguments' to robustness checks against defined philosophical problems (e.g., personal identity, moral agency) and comparative analysis across a corpus, moving beyond singular interpretation to produce generalizable claims about the argumentative capacity of narrative forms.

ideas.title: Cross-Cultural Absence Analysis via Comparative Genomics Framework
ideas.description: This expansion draws an analogy from comparative genomics, where researchers study gene loss and conserved deletions across diverse species to understand evolutionary adaptation. In that field, the systematic comparison of what is missing—not just what is present—across distantly related organisms reveals deep functional constraints and convergent responses to shared environmental pressures. We adapt this framework to comparative literary analysis by treating non-Western literary traditions (e.g., Lu Xun in Chinese literature, Natsume Sōseki in Japanese literature, Rabindranath Tagore in Bengali literature, Chinua Achebe in Igbo/Nigerian literature) as distinct cultural 'lineages' that independently confronted the 'selective pressure' of modernity—colonialism, industrialization, epistemic displacement. Using computational methods analogous to genome alignment (topic modeling, structured absence detection via expected-but-missing n-grams, and cross-corpus differential analysis), we systematically catalog what each tradition omits, suppresses, or circumnavigates when engaging with modernity. Convergent absences—themes independently silenced across multiple unrelated traditions—would suggest universal structural constraints that modernity imposes on literary expression, while divergent absences would reveal culturally specific coping mechanisms. This approach transforms absence analysis from a case-study method into a scalable comparative science of literary silence.

ideas.title: Multi-layer physiological-structural early warning system for forest die-off combining sub-canopy stress signals with crown density dynamics
ideas.description: This system overcomes the fundamental limitation of crown-density-only early warning by integrating leading physiological stress indicators (solar-induced fluorescence, vegetation optical depth, and dendrometer growth anomalies) with crown density as a confirmatory structural signal. A statistical framework based on critical slowing down theory monitors rising autocorrelation and variance across all indicator streams, triggering alerts when multiple independent signals cross pre-calibrated thresholds simultaneously. The approach is validated against documented die-off events across biomes to establish how much additional lead time the multi-layer index provides over crown density alone. By treating crown thinning as a late-stage confirmation rather than the primary warning signal, the system shifts detection from documenting ongoing mortality to forecasting incipient physiological collapse.

ideas.title: Context-Aware Computational Modeling of Rhetorical Absence in Early Modern Corpora
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid computational-hermeneutic system to model the nuanced construction of absence in early modern literature. It moves beyond bag-of-words analysis by combining contextual embedding models, supervised learning on expert annotations, and contrastive topic analysis to identify both explicit and implicit rhetorical strategies of omission, negation, and void. The system is designed with an iterative feedback loop, where quantitative results are continuously interpreted and challenged by domain-specific scholarship to ensure historical and theoretical validity. This approach aims to produce a robust, scalable, and critically engaged analysis of absence as a complex literary phenomenon.

ideas.title: Isotope-resolved carbon starvation mapping under heat-driven vapor pressure deficit stress
ideas.description: This fused approach applies stable carbon isotope pulse-labeling to trees subjected to extreme heat treatments without precipitation reduction, directly testing whether temperature-driven vapor pressure deficit (VPD) stress alone redirects carbon allocation in ways that lead to lethal carbon starvation. By tracking 13C-labeled photoassimilates in real time under controlled heat-only versus combined heat-drought conditions, the method can disentangle whether high-temperature VPD stress starves tissues by forcing stomatal closure and reducing photosynthesis, by rerouting carbon toward osmotic adjustment at the expense of storage reserves, or both. This provides the first mechanistic carbon budget for heat-only mortality pathways, resolving the long-standing debate about whether carbon starvation or hydraulic failure dominates temperature-driven die-off. The fusion creates a uniquely powerful diagnostic framework: isotope tracing supplies the mechanistic resolution that observational temperature-mortality correlations lack, while the heat-only experimental design eliminates the confound of precipitation deficit that has plagued previous isotope labeling studies conducted under combined drought.

ideas.title: Epidemiological surveillance anomaly detection adapted for forest die-off monitoring
ideas.description: This research transfers the statistical framework of public health syndromic surveillance to the domain of remote sensing phenology for forest die-off detection. In epidemiology, outbreak detection systems monitor streams of health indicators against learned seasonal baselines and trigger alerts when deviations exceed statistical thresholds, using methods such as CUSUM, EWMA, and Kulldorff's spatial scan statistic. The analogous problem—detecting emerging forest die-off from satellite imagery—shares key structural features: noisy spatiotemporal time series with strong seasonal periodicity, the need to distinguish true anomalies from normal interannual variability, and the requirement for spatially explicit cluster detection across large heterogeneous regions. The adapted approach constructs per-pixel phenological baselines from multi-year MODIS/Sentinel-2 NDVI and EVI archives, characterizing expected green-up onset, peak greenness, senescence timing, and seasonal amplitude. CUSUM and EWMA control charts are then applied to each pixel's phenological metrics to detect persistent negative deviations indicative of canopy decline. Simultaneously, spatial scan statistics identify emerging clusters of anomalous pixels, distinguishing localized die-off events from region-wide drought stress. The system would operate in near-real-time using 5–16 day satellite revisit composites, analogous to weekly epidemiological reporting cycles. Validation against known die-off events in Mediterranean forests (e.g., Quercus ilex and Pinus halepensis mortality documented in Spain and southern France) would benchmark detection sensitivity and lead time compared to conventional change-detection approaches. This cross-disciplinary transfer leverages decades of refinement in outbreak detection methodology to address a growing ecological crisis with improved statistical rigor and earlier warning capability.

ideas.title: Temporal cascade mapping of coupled hydraulic-carbon failure dynamics via continuous in-vivo sensing and pathway-specific perturbation
ideas.description: This approach deploys continuous-logging microdendrometers, embedded stem psychrometers, and sap flow sensors alongside repeated minimally-invasive microcoring for non-structural carbohydrate profiling across roots, stems, and canopy organs to capture the temporal ordering of hydraulic and carbon stress signals at sub-daily resolution during drought. Critically, it incorporates pathway-specific manipulation experiments—phloem girdling to isolate carbon transport effects from hydraulic status, and air-injection embolism induction to degrade hydraulic conductivity without directly altering carbon pools—enabling causal inference about which failure mode initiates mortality cascades. A dynamic Bayesian network model integrates the multivariate time-series data to quantify directed coupling strengths and lag structures between hydraulic dysfunction and carbon depletion across species with contrasting drought strategies. This design overcomes the fundamental confound of snapshot-based partitioning by establishing temporal precedence and causal directionality in the inherently coupled hydraulic-carbon failure system.

ideas.title: Dual-failure partitioning in tree mortality via cardiology-inspired differential diagnostics
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy between the challenge of partitioning hydraulic failure and carbon starvation in tree drought mortality and the well-established clinical problem of differentiating ischemic from metabolic heart failure in cardiology. In medicine, differential diagnosis of cardiac failure modes is achieved by simultaneously measuring vascular flow capacity (coronary flow reserve via perfusion MRI) and cellular energy status (metabolic biomarkers, FDG-PET imaging), then applying multi-criteria decision frameworks to assign relative causation. Adapting this approach, we propose a dual-diagnostic protocol for trees: hydraulic status is continuously monitored via sapflow sensors, stem psychrometry, and acoustic emissions that detect cavitation events (analogous to vascular imaging), while metabolic status is tracked through repeated sampling of non-structural carbohydrates, starch reserves, and carbon isotope discrimination in sapwood and phloem tissues (analogous to metabolic biomarkers). Trees are subjected to controlled progressive drought as a 'stress test,' and time-series data from both measurement streams are integrated using multivariate classification models—akin to clinical decision trees—to determine whether each individual tree crosses the hydraulic failure threshold, the carbon starvation threshold, or both, and in what temporal sequence. This framework moves beyond the current binary debate by providing a quantitative, continuous partitioning of failure modes, borrowing the proven logic of differential diagnostics from cardiovascular medicine.

ideas.title: Adapting high-frequency financial volatility modeling for intra-annual tree growth dynamics
ideas.description: Annual tree-ring measurements aggregate an entire season of growth into a single datum, obscuring the timing, intensity, and duration of drought impacts on cambial activity. This parallels a well-known problem in quantitative finance: annual returns mask intra-year volatility regimes, crash events, and momentum shifts that are critical for risk management. Financial econometricians solved this by developing realized volatility estimators and stochastic volatility state-space models (e.g., SV models estimated via sequential Monte Carlo / particle filters) that extract meaningful signal from noisy, high-frequency price data. We adapt this framework to dendroecology by treating continuous dendrometer band measurements as a high-frequency 'price series' of stem radius, where the observed signal is a mixture of irreversible radial growth (the 'trend return'), reversible bark water-status fluctuations (the 'microstructure noise'), and episodic drought-induced growth cessations (analogous to 'volatility regime switches'). Specifically, we propose a Bayesian hierarchical state-space model in which: (1) a latent growth-rate process follows a regime-switching dynamic driven by soil moisture thresholds, (2) a separate hydration state captures diurnal and weather-driven stem shrinkage-swelling cycles, and (3) observation error accounts for sensor noise. Wavelet-based pre-filtering, borrowed from financial tick-data cleaning, separates sub-daily noise from weekly growth signals. The model is fitted to dendrometer and xylogenesis data from drought-manipulation experiments using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo, yielding posterior estimates of weekly wood formation rates, drought response onset and recovery times, and species-specific sensitivity thresholds—all at temporal resolutions impossible from traditional ring-width chronologies.

ideas.title: Satellite-dendrometer fusion framework for spatially scalable intra-annual tree growth monitoring under drought
ideas.description: This approach deploys automated point dendrometers at stratified reference sites spanning key species and aridity gradients to capture continuous sub-weekly radial stem growth, then uses these time series as training targets for random forest and temporal convolutional network models that ingest co-located satellite remote sensing streams (Sentinel-1 radar backscatter, MODIS NDVI/EVI, and TROPOMI solar-induced fluorescence). The trained models predict weekly radial increment across the full satellite footprint, enabling drought-growth response mapping at landscape to regional scales without requiring dendrometers at every location. Cross-validation against withheld dendrometer sites and independent xylogenesis cores quantifies prediction uncertainty and identifies biome-specific transferability limits. This fusion framework overcomes the fundamental scalability bottleneck of plot-level xylogenesis studies while retaining their high temporal resolution advantage over tree-ring approaches.

ideas.title: Self-supervised anomaly detection in dendrochronological networks with ecological priors
ideas.description: This system pretrains a temporal encoder on thousands of unlabeled tree-ring chronologies using masked-segment prediction, learning species- and climate-aware representations of normal radial growth trajectories without requiring mortality labels. A lightweight anomaly detection head then identifies pre-mortality growth departures as sequences that deviate significantly from the encoder's learned expectations, sidestepping the critical data scarcity and overfitting problems of direct supervised RNN classification. Domain knowledge is injected through auxiliary climate and competition covariates that constrain the latent space, ensuring the model distinguishes genuine stress-induced growth decline from climate-driven variability. Generalization is validated through leave-one-site-out evaluation across diverse forest types, providing rigorous evidence that detected anomaly patterns transfer beyond the training distribution.

ideas.title: Landscape Transition Pathways via Markov Chain Monte Carlo Simulation
ideas.description: This research adapts the path-sampling approach from computational chemistry to model landscape transitions. The core idea is to simulate the stepwise temporal trajectory from a current to a potential future landscape by treating the process as a Markov chain of probabilistic state transitions between landscape types. Drawing on the analogous solution from molecular dynamics—where MCMC methods reveal probable conformational pathways—we propose a framework that uses transition probability matrices (derived from historical data and projected drivers) within an MCMC simulation to generate an ensemble of possible transition pathways. This moves beyond comparing two static snapshots to explicitly model the sequence, timing, and probability of intermediate states, thereby identifying critical transition thresholds, resilient pathways, and the inherent uncertainty in landscape evolution.

ideas.title: Radiotracer pharmacokinetic modeling adapted for plant carbon flux partitioning under drought
ideas.description: In nuclear medicine, radiotracer pharmacokinetics uses compartmental models to track how labeled substrates (such as 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose) are distributed, metabolized, and cleared across body tissues over time. PET imaging combined with two- or three-compartment kinetic models yields precise transfer rate constants between blood plasma, interstitial fluid, and intracellular metabolic pools. This framework was designed to solve the exact problem of tracking a labeled resource as it flows through competing biological sinks in real time. We adapt this approach to plant carbon physiology by applying 13C-CO2 pulse-labeling and treating the plant-soil system as a series of interconnected compartments: source leaves, phloem transport, stem storage, root biomass, mycorrhizal networks, and soil exudate pools. Time-series sampling of 13C enrichment in each compartment, combined with pharmacokinetic-style ordinary differential equation models, allows estimation of transfer rate constants between compartments under well-watered versus drought conditions. This reveals not just where carbon ends up, but the dynamic reprioritization of allocation fluxes — for example, whether drought accelerates carbon transfer to roots and mycorrhizae at the expense of stem storage, and over what timescale these shifts occur. The pharmacokinetic framework provides a rigorous, quantitative alternative to simple mass-balance approaches, enabling sensitivity analysis and model selection to identify which allocation pathways are most responsive to water deficit.

ideas.title: Continuous biomarker depletion monitoring adapted from glycogen tracking in exercise physiology
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy between glycogen depletion monitoring in exercise physiology and non-structural carbohydrate (NSC) dynamics in drought-stressed trees. In sports science, longitudinal tracking of muscle glycogen across repeated exercise bouts revealed that individual athletes have reproducible depletion thresholds below which performance catastrophically declines. Researchers developed protocols combining invasive sampling with non-invasive physiological proxies to predict when these thresholds would be reached. Adapting this framework, we propose a longitudinal NSC monitoring system for trees subjected to repeated drought cycles. Periodic destructive micro-core sampling of phloem, sapwood, and fine roots would be paired with continuous non-invasive measurements—dendrometer-based stem shrinkage, sap flow velocity, predawn water potential, and pulse-amplitude modulated chlorophyll fluorescence—to construct individual-tree NSC depletion trajectories. Machine learning models trained on these paired datasets would identify non-invasive proxy signatures that reliably predict when NSC reserves approach lethal thresholds, enabling early-warning detection of trees at imminent risk of carbon starvation-induced mortality without requiring destructive sampling. This cross-disciplinary approach transforms episodic NSC snapshots into continuous predictive monitoring, analogous to how wearable sensors now predict glycogen depletion in endurance athletes.

ideas.title: Adapting cardiology's ECG arrhythmia detection framework for pre-mortality growth anomaly recognition in tree-ring chronologies
ideas.description: This research adapts the proven framework of ECG-based cardiac arrhythmia detection to the dendrochronological domain for automated identification of pre-mortality growth anomalies in tree-ring time series. In cardiology, LSTM and GRU networks with temporal attention mechanisms have achieved high accuracy in detecting subtle pre-arrest patterns in quasi-periodic ECG signals. Tree-ring chronologies share fundamental structural similarities with ECG recordings: both are sequential biological signals with quasi-periodic structure, baseline variability across individuals, and characteristic anomaly signatures preceding critical failure events (cardiac arrest or tree death). The adapted architecture uses multi-scale temporal convolutions to capture both individual ring-width anomalies (analogous to beat-level features) and longer-term growth trajectory shifts (analogous to rhythm-level features). An attention layer highlights the temporal windows most predictive of mortality, providing interpretable outputs that align with known ecophysiological decline mechanisms such as hydraulic failure and carbon starvation. Transfer learning strategies borrowed from cross-patient ECG generalization enable models trained on extensively sampled species (e.g., Pinus ponderosa) to be fine-tuned for species with limited mortality-labeled chronologies. This cross-domain methodology promises both improved detection sensitivity over traditional statistical approaches like growth-trend analysis and novel ecological insights into the temporal dynamics of tree decline.
